2024
UNIVERSAL
REGISTRATION
DOCUMENT
Including the Annual Financial Report
The Universal Registration Document in French was filed on March 28, 2025 with the French financial markets authority (Autorité des
marchés financiers – AMF) as competent authority under Regulation (EU) 2017/1129, without prior approval pursuant to Article 9 of said
Regulation. The Universal Registration Document may be used for the purposes of an offer to the public of securities or admission of
securities to trading on a regulated market together with any amendments, if applicable, and a securities note and summary
approved by the AMF in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2017/1129. The English language version of this report is a free translation from the
original, which was prepared in French. In all matters of interpretation, views or opinions expressed in the original language version
of the document in French take precedence over the translation.
The annual financial report included in the Universal Registration Document is a translation of the official version of the annual
financial report in format ESEF (European Single Electronic Format) and is available on the issuer’s website. This document is a
translation into English of the Universal Registration Document.

Our core purpose
“ Thanks to the commitment
of our employees, proven

develop solutions that meet
strategic needs, such as
defense and access to space.”

## Page 6

Editorial
With the conviction that the challenges involved in decarbonizing
the aviation industry require a strong combination of innovation and
responsibility, the Board of Directors endeavors to uphold the Group’s
technological leadership, investing today for sustainable growth that
meets the industry's decarbonization objectives.” 
Ross McInnes — Chairman of the Board of Directors
“The Safran success draws on talent, innovation, operational
excellence, cutting-edge technologies and an absolute priority on
quality and safety. This puts the Group in a strong position from
which to drive a sustainable change in aviation, and above all meet
the needs of our partners and customers.
Olivier Andriès — Chief Executive Officer
“
„

Editorial

SAFRAN    2024 UNIVERSAL REGISTRATION DOCUMENT

## Page 7

INTRODUCTION
Editorial
afran achieved exceptional financial
performance in 2024, with revenue,
recurring operating income and free
cash
flow
reaching
unprecedented

levels. These results, in line with or
ahead of forecasts, confirm the robust-
ness of our business model in a global
context of persistent supply-chain difficulties and

geopolitical uncertainties.
In 2024, we celebrated a historic milestone with the 50th
anniversary of CFM International, our emblematic part-
nership with GE Aerospace. This marks five decades of
success and innovation at the service of global aviation.
Achievements on the commercial front in 2024 included
strategic contracts with easyJet, Akasa Air and American
Airlines. The success of the LEAP engine strengthens our
position as world leader in short- and medium-haul propul-
sion. The order backlog stands at over 11,500 engines, and
the LEAP fleet is growing twice as fast as that of the CFM56
engine.
Significant new program milestones included the entry
into service of the Gulfstream G700, equipped with
Safran nacelles, and the Airbus A321XLR, equipped
with LEAP engines. This progress illustrates our ability
to meet the needs of a fast-changing sector. In addition,
the certification of two new helicopter engines in China
extended our presence in strategic high-growth markets.
In the defense and space sectors, Safran consolidated its
role as a strategic partner. Our contribution to European
sovereignty was illustrated by the maiden flight of Ariane 6,
Serbia's order for Rafale, and delivery of the first produc-
tion Patroller drone to the French army. In addition, our
SkyJacker anti-drone solution played a key role in ensuring
security at the Paris 2024 Olympic and Paralympic Games.
We are continuing to move ahead on our decarbonization
trajectory. Inauguration of the BeCOVER testbed in Bel-
gium for full-scale testing of RISE compressors and wind-
tunnel testing for our Open Fan engine, mark a key step
towards more sustainable aviation. Recent EASA certifica-
tion for the ENGINeUS 100 electric motor, a world first, also
testifies to our commitment to pushing the boundaries of
technological innovation.
Our digital transformation efforts also made major
advances in 2024. Our capabilities in cutting-edge tech-
nologies were further strengthened by the launch of ACE,
our embedded AI solution for defense applications, and
the integration of Safran.AI following the acquisition of Pre-
ligens.
At Safran’s Capital Markets Day (CMD) in 2024, the Group
presented its financial and strategic ambitions for 2028, and
its main objectives for the coming years: a smooth transi-
tion from CFM56 to LEAP engines, the ramp-up of original
equipment and MRO activities, and a targeted innovation
strategy for decarbonization. Safran has also reaffirmed
its objective of increasing returns to its shareholders while
investing massively in the technologies of the future.
With the rollout of "Safran Together", our new behavioral
framework rooted in trust, innovation and commitment,
we reassert our determination to shape an ambitious and
respectful future for the Group and its employees.
In 2025, Safran will be celebrating its twentieth
anniversary and the centenary of Sagem, now Safran
Electronics & Defense. A key event for the aerospace
sector in 2025 will be the Paris Air Show, where we will be
spotlighting our latest innovations and our contribution
to the future of sustainable aerospace. This major event
provides a valuable showcase for our technological
leadership and will allow us to demonstrate our
commitment to building a sustainable future, remaining true
to our rich heritage. We are determined to keep ramping up
production, recruiting the best talent, and above all meeting
our customers’ expectations.
We would like to thank you
for your trust and hope you enjoy
reading this report.
Ross McInnes and Olivier Andriès
S
MESSAGE from the Chairman
of the Board of Directors and
the Chief Executive Officer

## Page 9

INTRODUCTION
Presentation of the Group
PROPULSION
A fully-fledged engine manufacturer,(1) Safran supplies airframers
with engines for commercial aircraft, military aircraft and helicopters.
To increase cost efficiency and share risks, engine manufacturers often develop
their engine programs in partnership. Safran has notably partnered with GE
Aerospace since the 1970s, when they set up the 50/50 joint venture CFM
International that has developed the CFM56® and LEAP® engines.
The partnership has been extended through to 2050. Safran also contributes to
space access through its 50% stake in the ArianeGroup joint venture.

AIRCRAFT INTERIORS
To ensure passenger safety and optimize comfort, Safran develops cabin
interiors, passenger and crew seats, water and waste management systems,
in-flight entertainment systems (RAVE™), and interior retrofit for commercial
aircraft. The Aircraft Interiors business addresses both airframers and operators.
EQUIPMENT & DEFENSE
Safran is one of the world’s leading suppliers of safety and other mission critical
equipment for aerospace and defense applications: landing, steering and braking
systems; nacelles; electrical systems; and engineering solutions. Safran’s
Aerosystems division also offers safety systems (slides, oxygen masks, on-board
oxygen generation, etc.) and fluid management systems.
In the defense sector, Safran offers a diversified portfolio of products adapted to
battlefield transformation: optronics, avionics, navigation, tactical drones,
electronics and critical software.
In 2024, the Group acquired Preligens, now Safran.AI, to develop new applications
of artificial intelligence in aerospace and defense.

production capabilities.

1905
Société des Moteurs Gnome
is founded in the Paris suburb
of Gennevilliers.
Gnome rotary engines
become the standard
for planes around the world.
1912
Creation of Société des Moteurs
Le Rhône, Gnome’s main competitor
before being taken over by its rival.
1925
Creation of Société d’Applications
Générales d’Électricité et de
Mécanique (Sagem) that will mainly
manufacture cameras and artillery
equipment and go on to design
the world’s first infrared guidance
system for air-to-air missiles.
1945
Gnome & Rhône is nationalized
and renamed Snecma
(Société Nationale d’Étude et de
Construction de Moteurs d’Aviation).
1945-2002
Several aerospace companies join
Snecma: Hispano-Suiza, a specialist
in power transmission for aircraft
engines, followed by Messier-Hispano-
Bugatti, a specialist in landing gear.
In 2000, wiring specialist Labinal and
its helicopter engine manufacturer
subsidiary Turbomeca join Snecma.
In 2002, nacelles specialist
Hurel‑Dubois joins Snecma.
1974
Snecma becomes a civil aircraft
engine manufacturer through
a cooperation agreement
with GE for the manufacture
of the CFM56 engine.

## Page 25

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Safran overview
1.1.3
Main Group companies by business sector
Safran today covers three main markets: Propulsion, Equipment and Defense, and Aircraft Interiors.
Propulsion
Safran Aircraft Engines
Engines for civil and military aircraft. Capabilities covering the
entire product cycle, from design, development and production
though to support, services, maintenance and repair.
Safran Transmission Systems
High-performance power transmission systems for civil and
military airplane and helicopter engines, as well as for new air
mobility solutions, with accessory drive trains and gearboxes, and
turbofan, turboprop and hybrid propulsion reduction gearboxes.
Capabilities covering the entire lifecycle of power transmission
systems, from design and production to maintenance, repair and
overhaul (MRO). Mechanical components for airplane and helicopter
propulsion systems.
Safran Aero Boosters
Low-pressure compressors and lubrication equipment for aircraft
engines. Test cells and equipment for engine testing. Regulation
valves for space engines.
Safran Helicopter Engines
Engines for aircraft (chiefly civil, semi-public and military helicopters),
auxiliary power units (APUs), starting systems for civil and
military aircraft, and propulsion systems for missiles, target
drones and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). Maintenance, repair
and overhaul (MRO), and support and related services.
Equipment & Defense
Safran Landing Systems
Aircraft landing, braking and taxiing systems. Related control and
monitoring systems. Capabilities covering the entire product
cycle, from design and production to maintenance, repair and
overhaul (MRO).
Safran Nacelles
Aircraft nacelles for all civil aviation market segments: commercial
aircraft of more than 100 seats, regional and business jets.
Proficiency in systems integration, aerodynamics, acoustics and
composite and metal materials, with capabilities covering the
entire product cycle, from design and production to maintenance,
repair and overhaul (MRO).
Safran Electrical & Power
Electrical power systems for the aerospace market, covering
all  onboard electrical functions (power generation, distribution
and conversion, wiring, components, load management). Electric
engine systems and hybridization of propulsion systems.
Engineering solutions.
Safran Aerosystems
Systems for aircraft and helicopters: safety, evacuation and
flotation systems, oxygen systems, fuel management and
distribution systems, and fluid management systems. Capabilities
covering the entire product cycle, from design and production to
maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO).
Safran Electronics & Defense
Equipment, systems and services that deliver intelligence to the
civil and military programs they equip and allow them to assess
environments (observe), ease cognitive load (decide) and
guarantee trajectories (guide): optronics, high‑performance
optics,
RF
systems,
avionics,
navigation,
electronics,
electromechanics, electric propulsion, critical software and
drones. Land, sea, air and space applications. Customer
support for all applications.
Aircraft Interiors
Safran Cabin
Cabin interiors for regional, medium-haul, long-haul, business
and military aircraft. Integrated cabins, overhead bins, galleys,
lavatories and crew rest areas. Environment control and ventilation
systems, water and waste management systems, lighting and
cabin electronics management systems, and engineering services
for cabin interior refits for commercial and private aircraft.
Capabilities covering the entire product cycle, from design and
production to maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO).
Safran Seats
Passenger seats for economy, premium economy, business
and first class, technical seats for flight-deck and cabin crew,
helicopter seats. Capabilities covering the entire product cycle, from
design and production to maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO).

## Page 26

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Safran overview
1.1.4
Organization and position of the issuer in the Group
Organization
Safran is an industrial group, within which each tier-one subsidiary
directly manages the operational aspects of its business activities,
both its own and those of its subsidiaries, and is responsible for
implementing internal control systems in accordance with Group
procedures and internal rules.
The organizational structure is based on:
◼Safran, the parent company and issuer, responsible for the
Group’s strategic management, organization and development;
◼companies handling specific business lines, under strategies
determined by the parent company’s Board of Directors.
Executive Management of the parent company ensures that
the strategic orientations defined for each business line are
implemented and complied with at the operational level.
Tier-one entities (shown in section 1.1.2) are responsible for
overseeing the subsidiaries with which they have operational ties.
Role of the issuer within the Group
As the Group’s parent company, Safran performs the following
functions for the Group companies:
◼it holds and manages shares in the Group subsidiaries;
◼it leads the Group and drives its growth by (i) determining the
Group’s strategic vision, including the sustainability strategy;
(ii) defining the research and technology (R&T) policy, tax
policy, legal and financial policy, quality, purchasing and
industrial performance policies and human resources policy;
and (iii) overseeing employee training, retraining and skills
matching by Safran University, communications and operations
control;
◼in particular, it provides Group companies with:
●support services on legal, taxation and financial matters,
and in particular:
– centralized cash pooling to manage cash needs and
surpluses more effectively across the Group,
– foreign currency management policy to reduce uncertainty
factors and protect the economic performance of
operating subsidiaries from random foreign currency
fluctuations (mainly US dollars),
– tax consolidation, in jurisdictions such as France where
Safran is liable for the entire income tax charge, additional
income tax contributions and the minimal tax charge
due  by the tax group comprising itself and its
tax‑consolidated subsidiaries;
●services through Shared Services Centers (SSCs) in France in
the following areas: payroll administration and management,
recruitment, non-production purchases, materials purchases,
IT, and some transaction accounting (customers, suppliers
and fixed assets).
Financial flows between the issuer and Group companies
Safran receives dividends paid by its subsidiaries in compliance
with applicable regulations (see section 3.3, Note 4.4).
It receives payment for services provided to Group companies,
and invoices them for services provided through the SSCs (see
section 3.3, Note 4.1).
1.1.5
Strategic priorities
Safran's strategic priorities for generating earnings and cash
flow growth in the medium term and ensuring a fair return to
shareholders are as follows:
◼absolute priority: product safety and quality above all;
◼strengthening supply chain resilience;
◼effectively managing the ramp-up for original equipment and
MRO (Maintenance, Repair and Overhaul);
◼ensuring the smooth transition of aftermarket services
between CFM56® and LEAP® engines;
◼accelerating growth in the Defense and Space equipment
business in a changing military context;
◼leveraging innovation to give Safran a keystone position in the
decarbonization effort and preparing for the next generation
of aircraft;
◼maintaining a disciplined investment policy to prepare for the
future;
◼pursuing a controlled, targeted M&A strategy and streamlining
the portfolio.

Group businesses
1.2
GROUP BUSINESSES 
1.2.1
Propulsion
A full-fledged engine manufacturer(1), Safran supplies airframers with engines for commercial aircraft, military aircraft and helicopters.
To  increase cost efficiency and share risks, engine manufacturers often develop their engine programs in partnership. Safran has
partnered with GE Aerospace (United States) since the 1970s, when they set up the 50/50 joint venture CFM International, which
develops the CFM56 and LEAP engines. This partnership has been extended through to 2050. Safran also contributes to access to
space through its 50% stake in the ArianeGroup joint venture.
This business comprises four main segments:

## Page 28

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses 
1.2.1.1
Aircraft engines
Civil aircraft engines
Key characteristics of the business sector
In response to airframers’ requirements for specific engines for
civil aircraft, engine manufacturers invest in engine programs
comprising two types of activity:
◼original equipment, involving the sale of engines installed on
new aircraft;
◼aftermarket, comprising the sale of spare parts and service
contracts entered into on a case-by-case or long-term basis
with operators and approved maintenance centers.
ECONOMIC LIFE CYCLE OF AN AIRCRAFT ENGINE PROGRAM(1)
In addition to the engine program lifecycle as such, we have research and technology (R&T) expenses, mostly self-financed, which begin several
years upstream of development.
These programs may span more than 40 years and have several
phases. A significant portion of revenue generated by these
programs comes from aftermarket services. The US dollar is
practically the exclusive transaction currency in this sector,
which features four major engine manufacturer prime contractors:
GE Aerospace, Pratt & Whitney (United States), Rolls-Royce (United
Kingdom), and Safran.
Alliances and partnerships
Given the high degree of investment involved in each new
engine program, Safran often works in partnership with other
engine manufacturers, through joint ventures in some cases. In
1974, for example, CFM International was formed as a 50/50 joint
venture with GE Aerospace to develop engines for short- to
medium-haul aircraft. The partnership has been extended
through to 2050.
They can also be based on contractual risk-and-revenue-sharing
agreements, under which Safran receives a share of revenue
from the final delivered product and the sale of related spare
parts, corresponding to its share in the program. For example,
this kind of arrangement applies on high-thrust engine programs
such as CF6, GE90, GEnx, GP7200 and GE9X.
Group products and programs
The Group’s operations in the civil aircraft engines segment
mainly involve Safran Aircraft Engines and Safran Aero Boosters.

## Page 29

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses
Low-thrust civil engines (8,000 to 18,000 pounds
of thrust)
In this engine range, which concerns regional aircraft (36 to
100  seats) and business jets, Safran operations involve
participation in two main programs:
◼CF34-10 (9.3%), for Embraer 190 and COMAC C909 regional jets;
◼Passport (7.4%) for Bombardier business jets Global  7500
and Global 8000 (Canada).
Safran also offers its Silvercrest engine for business jets. The
maturity demonstration phase for this engine, which incorporates
innovative technologies, is now complete.
Medium-power civil engines (18,000 to 50,000 pounds
of thrust)
For short- and medium-haul aircraft (100 to over 200 seats), the
Group's involvement in the CFM56 and LEAP programs has
developed under cooperation agreements by CFM International,
a 50/50 joint venture between Safran and GE Aerospace.
NUMBER OF PRODUCTION-STANDARD CFM56 AND LEAP ENGINES MANUFACTURED
CFM56 engines power single-aisle commercial aircraft from
Airbus (A320ceo family)(1) and Boeing (United States) (Boeing 737
Classic and Boeing 737 NG families). This is the world's
best‑selling engine today, with close to 34,000 units delivered
and an operating base(2) of around 31,700 engines (including
some 23,000 CFM56-5B/7B second generation engines, which
generate the majority of aftermarket activities for this program).
LEAP engines have so far been selected for use on three aircraft:
◼the LEAP-1A version is one of the two engine options for the
new Airbus A320neo aircraft family, the other being the
PurePower PW1000G developed by Pratt & Whitney;
◼the LEAP-1B version is the sole engine for the Boeing 737 MAX;
◼the LEAP-1C version, as the sole Western source for the
propulsion system (engine plus nacelle) on China-based
COMAC’s C919 aircraft.
In 2024, the CFM56 and LEAP engine programs (including spare
parts, maintenance and repair sales) generated 66% of Safran’s
Propulsion revenue.
High-power civil engines (over 50,000 pounds of thrust)
In this engine range, the Group operates as a risk and revenue
sharing partner of GE Aerospace. Safran's stake ranges from
7% to 24% in several engine programs currently in series
production, including:
◼the CF6-80C powering the Boeing 767 (10%), and the GE90
powering the Boeing 777 (23.7% for the GE90-115 version
currently in production);
◼the GEnX (7.7%) for version 1B on Boeing 787 long-haul
aircraft, and the future GE9X engine (11.2%), the exclusive
engine for the new Boeing 777X long-haul aircraft, scheduled
to enter service in 2026.
Safran is also involved in programs that now generate only spare
parts and repair business, such as the GP7200 engine (17.5%)
powering the A380, the CF6-80E (19.4%) powering the A330,
and the GEnX-2B (7.3%) powering the Boeing 747-8.
(1)
Competition to power the Airbus A320ceo aircraft family comes from the V2500 engine made by the IAE consortium (Pratt & Whitney, MTU Aero Engines
in Germany and Japanese Aero Engines Corp in Japan).
(2)
Engines in operation equals engines delivered (including engines in storage) less engines dismantled or scrapped.

## Page 30

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses 
Safran is involved in the LM6000 gas turbine program (related to
the CF6 engines), both in production and aftersales support,
with a stake ranging from 4.4% to 15.9% depending on the
turbine version. On the LM9000 gas turbine program (for the
GE90 engine), Safran supplies the high-pressure compressor
and associated spare parts.
Spare parts and services for civil aircraft engines
Aftermarket operations primarily involve the sale of spare parts
and the provision of maintenance and repair services. Spare
parts are sold directly to third-party service centers. For services,
contracts are invoiced by flight hour, providing airlines with
long‑term visibility for their engine maintenance expenditure.
Military aircraft engines
Key characteristics of the business sector
The military aviation sector comprises three main segments:
◼fighters;
◼training and support aircraft;
◼patrol, tanker and transport aircraft.
As in the civil aviation sector, military aircraft engine programs
break down into three phases. The first phase is the engine
development and qualification phase. It is followed by the
production phase, which involves equipping the newly
manufactured aircraft. The third phase is the longest and
involves the aftermarket, which comprises the sale of spare
parts, maintenance and repair activities, plus other customer
services through to the end of the engine service life.
In the military engine market, the four Western end-to-end
engine manufacturers are: Safran, GE Aerospace, Rolls-Royce
and Pratt & Whitney. Alliances and partnerships between engine
manufacturers can be formed to bring together the best
technological and commercial assets, share the risks involved in
development, and meet the needs of international programs.
Group products and programs
Safran’s operations in the military aircraft engines segment
mainly involve Safran Aircraft Engines and Safran Helicopter
Engines. Other Group companies, such as Safran Aero Boosters,
Safran Electronics & Defense, Safran Transmission Systems,
Safran Nacelles and Safran Aerosystems, are also involved
in  the military aircraft engine sector, through the production
of components.
Fighter engines
Historically linked to Dassault Aviation, Safran's operations in
this segment mainly involve the M88 program (7.5 metric tons of
thrust) powering the Rafale, the M53-P2 program (9.5
metric tons of thrust) powering all versions of the Mirage 2000,
and the ATAR program (5 to 7.1 metric tons of thrust) powering
the Mirage III, 5 and F1 families.
As part of the Future Combat Air System (FCAS) program(1)
undertaken by the French, German and Spanish governments,
Safran Aircraft Engines and MTU Aero Engines (Germany),
through their EUMET (EUropean Military Engine Team) joint
venture(2), are designing the engine for the New Generation
Fighter (NGF) with their partner ITP Aero (Spain). The
sixth‑generation twin-engine NGF developed by Dassault
Aviation and Airbus will come into service with the three
countries’ air forces in the 2040’s.
For the Pratt & Whitney F135 engine (17.8 metric tons of thrust)
powering the Lockheed Martin F35 military aircraft, Safran has
signed two MoU (Memorandum of Understanding) agreements
with this American engine manufacturer: on a research and
technology project for production of structural parts, and on a
new manufacturing technology for engine components.
Training and support aircraft engines
Safran's activity in this segment is based on the following
programs:
◼the Adour engine (2.3 to 3.7 metric tons of thrust), which now
powers primarily the BAE Systems Hawk (United Kingdom)
and the Boeing T-45A Goshawk trainers;
◼the Larzac engine (1.4 metric tons of thrust), powering Dassault
Aviation’s twin-engined AlphaJet trainers (program generating
revenue solely through spare parts and repairs today).
Patrol, tanker and transport aircraft engines
Activity in this segment is based on the following programs:
◼the CFM56 engine powering military versions of the
Boeing 707, the Boeing KC135 tanker aircraft, the Boeing C-40
transport
aircraft,
the
Boeing
P-8
Poseidon
MMA
(Multimission Maritime Aircraft), and the Boeing AEW&C
(Airborne Early Warning & Control) aircraft;
◼the Tyne turboprop engine that powers Atlantique 2 maritime
patrol aircraft (program generating revenue solely through
spare parts and repairs today);
◼The 11,600 hp TP400 turboprop engine, to power the Airbus
A400M European military transport aircraft.
Spare parts and services for military aircraft engines
Aftermarket operations for military aircraft consist of new spare
parts, maintenance and repair for engines and parts, and user
support services. Safran thus offers customers contracts ranging
from the simple sale of spare parts through to comprehensive
services on operational fleet upkeep. These activities are directly
linked to the aircraft needs of governments using military aircraft.
(1)
The Future Combat Air System (FCAS) is a joint European program to develop a new-generation combat air system, combining a New Generation Fighter (NGF)
aircraft by 2040 with a wide array of interconnected and interoperable elements such as drones, and based on artificial intelligence.
(2)
50%-owned by each partner.

## Page 31

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses
1.2.1.2
Helicopter engines
Key characteristics of the business sector
In the helicopter engine market, we find wide diversity in
applications and end-users. The market splits into civilian fleets,
generally small in size, and larger military fleets.
Helicopter engine size is determined mainly by airframe weight
and mission type. Helicopters may have one, two or sometimes
three engines, which leads to a wide variety of engines and
related versions.
The Group addresses this diversity with applications in the
following segments:
◼government and semi-public: medical and emergency services,
police, firefighting and border control;
◼military: transportation, attack and ground support, rescue
and maritime patrol;
◼civil: airborne work (helicopter transport, refueling, civil
engineering), private transport and tourism, offshore oil.
The helicopter engine market, like the civil aviation market, has
two components:
◼sale of engines to helicopter manufacturers for installation in
new airframes (original equipment);
◼aftermarket activities based on spare part, maintenance and
repair contracts with operators and specialized companies,
requiring a major global customer support network with local
reach, given the large number of users and the helicopter’s
limited radius of action.
The majority of major Western aerospace manufacturers are also
present in the helicopter engine market: Safran, GE Aeropsace,
Pratt & Whitney Canada, Rolls-Royce and Honeywell (United
States). Safran ranks number one in the sector, with a market
share of 35%(1).
Group products and programs
Through Safran Helicopter Engines, Safran covers all of the
helicopter engine categories. With new hybrid electric systems
capable of powering the helicopters of the future, Safran
Helicopter Engines also contributes to the development of new
aircraft concepts and engine compatibility with SAF(2), the use of
which it is promoting in the helicopter market.
Safran also fields a range of auxiliary power units for missiles
and targets, through Safran Power Units. Auxiliary power units
are non-propulsive systems that generate energy on an aircraft
for powering onboard systems, on the ground in particular, as
well as starting engines.
Engines for light helicopters
For single-engine helicopters from 1.5 to 3.5 metric tons and
twin-engine helicopters from 2 to 5 metric tons, Safran offers
two engine families: Arrius (450 to 750 shp(3)) and Arriel (650 to
more than 1,000 shp).
Engines for medium-weight helicopters
For helicopters from four to six metric tons, Safran’s 1,300 shp
Arrano engine offers helicopter operators 15% lower fuel
consumption than the previous generation of engines in service
today.
For helicopters from 5 to 7 metric tons, Safran covers the
military market for combat helicopters, with the MTR390 (1,250
to 1,450 shp), developed jointly with Rolls-Royce, MTU Aero
Engines and ITP Aero.
For helicopters from 5 to 8 metric tons, Safran’s Ardiden range
of engines covers power ratings from 1,400 to 2,000 shp.
Engines for heavy-lift helicopters
On the market for heavy helicopters (9 to 15 metric tons), Safran
offers Makila engines (1,800 to 2,100 shp), RTM322 engines
(2,100  to 2,600 shp) and the new Aneto family of high-power
engines, with models covering a power range from 2,500 to over
3,000 shp.
Spare parts and services for helicopter engines
As in civil aviation, Safran has an international organization for
helicopter engines, offering technical and commercial support,
plus the sale of spare parts and hourly service contracts. Close
reach to customers is ensured by a worldwide network of
maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO) centers, which includes
sites run by Safran and by third-party partners.
New mobility concepts, hybrid propulsion systems
Safran has developed expertise in hybrid electric propulsion
systems, whose combined electric and thermal energy sources
make it possible both to optimize onboard power management
and to offer systems capable of powering emerging aircraft
concepts.
With its electrical equipment capabilities, the Group also
markets turbogenerators based on helicopter engines, which
also incorporate electrical equipment provided by Safran.
(1)
Percentage of new helicopters delivered in 2024 powered by Safran engines. Source: Safran.
(2)
SAF - Sustainable Aviation Fuel.
(3)
Shaft horsepower.

## Page 32

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses 
Auxiliary power units, turbojet engines for missiles
and target vehicles
The auxiliary power units developed by Safran meet the
demanding requirements of the world’s leading aircraft and
helicopter manufacturers. Safran equips a large fleet of civil and
military aircraft and helicopters. Through its R&T work on fuel
cells, Safran Power Units contributes to the development of
electric power generation solutions emitting zero CO2.
Through Safran Power Units, the Group is a leading global
player(1) in jet engines for military applications (missiles and
targets). Safran makes engines for missiles including the SCALP
and RBS-15, and for the US targets MQM-107 for the US Air
Force. In 2021, Safran also became involved in the British and
French joint FC/ASW program(2), working in 50/50 partnership with
Rolls-Royce to develop the engine for the next generation of
tactical missiles.
1.2.1.3
Mechanical power transmission systems
Key characteristics of the business sector
The main applications covered by the power transmission systems
market are:
◼accessory drive trains (ADTs), which supply the mechanical
energy needed for engine and aircraft equipment;
◼propeller gearboxes (PGBs) and reduction gearboxes (RGBs),
which transfer power to the turboprop propellers or the
high‑bypass-ratio turbofan;
◼main transmission gearboxes (MGBs), which transfer power
to the helicopter blades.
The main participants in this market are the engine and
helicopter manufacturers themselves, along with aerospace
equipment suppliers such as Collins Aerospace (Raytheon
Technologies Group - United States), Triumph (United States),
Avio Aero (Italy) and Honeywell Aerospace (United States).
Group products and programs
Safran’s operations in the mechanical power transmission
sector involve Safran Transmission Systems and the Aero
Gearbox International (AGI) joint venture with Rolls Royce
(United Kingdom).
The unit’s accessory drive trains power engines such as the
CFM56 (Airbus A320ceo and Boeing 737 NG), the LEAP (Airbus
A320neo, Boeing 737 MAX and COMAC C919), the Trent XWB
(Airbus A350), the M88 (Dassault Aviation Rafale) and the TP400
(Airbus Defence & Space A400M).
In addition, through the AGI joint venture, which designs,
develops and manufactures power transmission systems for
Rolls-Royce aircraft engines, Safran Transmission Systems
covers all the British engine-maker’s business and commercial
aircraft applications.
1.2.1.4
Launch vehicles – ArianeGroup (owned 50/50 by Safran and Airbus)
Key characteristics of the business sector
This business sector comprises three main segments:
◼the civil space industry, with launch vehicles for placing satellites
in orbit;
◼military applications, with tactical, ballistic and hypersonic
missiles;
◼products, equipment and services: a largely commercial
segment
covering
institutional
customers,
satellite
manufacturers and the launch vehicle industry.
In the first two segments, development and engineering
programs tend to follow long cycles under finance by institutional
budgets. European Space Agency (ESA) budgets are for civil
space activities in Europe, while French defense budgets are for
strategic operations.
In the civil space industry, competition is quite intense. The main
competitors of Arianespace (an ArianeGroup subsidiary) in the
commercial launch services market are SpaceX (United States)
with its reusable Falcon 9 launcher and the heavy-lift version
Falcon Heavy, United Launch Alliance (United States) with the
Vulcan Centaur launcher, the Indian Space Agency (ISRO(3)) with
the LVM 3 (Launch Vehicle Mark-3) and Mitsubishi Heavy
Industries (Japan) with the H3 launcher. Ariane 6 came into
service in 2024, a judicious move given that competition will be
intensifying in the medium-term, with a number of new launch
vehicles currently under development. These include New Glenn
from Blue Origin, Neutron from Rocket Lab, Terran R from
Relativity Space and MLV (Medium Launch Vehicle) from Firefly
Aerospace, all in the United States.
In this same segment, competitive pressure could also increase
in the near future with the arrival of new entrants specialized in
light payload launches. This is particularly the case in Europe,
with the development of micro-launchers in Germany (Rocket
Factory Augsburg’s RFA One and ISAR Aerospace’s Spectrum),
Spain (PLD Space’s Miura  5) and the United Kingdom (Orbex
Space’s Prime and Skyrora’s Skyrora XL). In response,
ArianeGroup, through MaiaSpace(4), is developing the Maia
mini‑launch vehicle, scheduled to make its first flight in 2026.
The military launch vehicle segment covers tactical, ballistic and
hypersonic missiles:
◼tactical missiles use various types of propulsion systems (solid
propellant, turbojet and ramjet). Safran’s main involvement in
this market is with turbojet propulsion, through Safran Power
Units. The other main player in the turbojet propulsion sector
is Williams International (United States);
◼in Europe, France is the only country to develop its own
ballistic missiles, for oceanic deterrent purposes, powered by
solid-propellant engines. The main Western players in the
ballistic missile segment are ArianeGroup in France and
Lockheed Martin and Northrop Grumman in the United States;
◼hypersonic missiles, currently in development, extend military
capabilities. The main players are Russia, China and the United
States. In France, the V-MAX (Véhicule Manœuvrant
eXpérimental,
or
experimental
maneuvering
vehicle)
demonstration program is the result of cooperation between
ArianeGroup and ONERA(5) on behalf of the French
Directorate General of Weapons Procurement (DGA).
(1)
Source: Safran.
(2)
Future Cruise and Anti-Ship Weapon.
(3)
ISRO - Indian Space Research Organisation.
(4)
ArianeGroup subsidiary.

## Page 33

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses
The third segment comprises products, equipment and
services  for civil and military launch vehicles, satellites, space
surveillance, production of critical infrastructures (remediation
units, destruction of First World War munitions, etc.), and
production of parts for the aerospace industry.
Group products and programs
Safran covers the civil and military launch vehicle sector through
ArianeGroup. ArianeGroup is the product of the joint ambition of
Safran and Airbus to drive the European space industry to
unrivaled heights, in a context of mounting international
competition. It merges both partners’ businesses and expertise
in civil and military launch vehicles.
Safran covers the sector of propulsion systems for tactical
missiles and targets through Safran Power Units.
Civil space industry
ArianeGroup is the prime contractor for the ESA on the future
Ariane 6 launch vehicles, which involves coordinating an
industrial network of more than 600 companies in 12 European
countries. It manages the whole of the industrial chain, which
covers plant and equipment, engine manufacture, and integration
of the launch vehicle at the Guiana Space Center.
ArianeGroup also contributes to solid propulsion for the Vega
program, through its joint interests with Avio in Europropulsion
and Regulus; the first stage of the Vega C launcher is common
with the boosters for Ariane 6.
Military space industry
Tactical missiles
Through Safran Power Units, the Group is a leading global
player(1) in jet engines for military applications (missiles and
targets). Safran makes engines for the SCALP, Exocet Block 3,
NSM(2) and RBS-15 missiles, and for the US targets BQM 167 and
177 used by the US Air Force and Navy. Safran also became
involved in the British and French joint FC/ASW program(3),
working in 50/50 partnership with Rolls-Royce to develop the
engine for the next generation of tactical missiles.
Ballistic missiles
In the military market, ArianeGroup is industrial prime contractor
on the missiles program for France’s fourth - generation
ocean‑going strategic nuclear force (M51), and its incremental
development. Neither ArianeGroup nor Safran is responsible for
the provision of nuclear warheads for these ballistic missiles.
ArianeGroup also develops technologies for forthcoming
high‑performance propulsion systems (modular propulsion,
DACS(4), etc.) under national and international production and
R&T contracts.
Hypersonic missiles
ArianeGroup is a key player in hypersonic weapons (at speeds
above Mach 5), developing the V-MAX (for Véhicule MAnœuvrant
eXpérimental) hypersonic glider demonstrator for the French
Armed Forces.
Products, equipment and services
ArianeGroup currently sells products, equipment and services
in  more than 50 countries, and supplies products such as
propulsion systems and antenna reflectors to the main
satellite  manufacturers. Through its worldwide Helix network,
ArianeGroup also meets the needs expressed by civil and
military customers for services involving the detection and
surveillance of space objects.
Other products and services come through ArianeGroup
subsidiaries:
◼Pyroalliance provides pyrotechnic equipment for a broad range
of missiles and launch vehicles (Ariane and Vega);
◼Sodern fields cutting-edge expertise in space instrumentation,
optics and neutronics for civil and military applications;
◼Nuclétudes provides prime expertise in tests and engineering
on ruggedization, for the protection of electronic systems and
mechanical structures in harsh radiative and electromagnetic
environments;
◼Aerospace Propulsion Products (APP) handles design,
development and production of engine initiators and gas
generators for space (Ariane and Vega) and industrial
applications.
Harnessing its experience in liquid hydrogen for launch vehicle
propulsion, ArianeGroup works with Safran to develop
technological solutions addressing the particular challenges of
liquid hydrogen, to be used for both aviation applications and for
other modes of transport.
(1)

(2)

(3)

(4)
Divert and Attitude Control System.

## Page 34

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses 
1.2.1.5
2024 highlights
Civil aircraft engines
Low-thrust engines: Safran contributed to work on GE's
Passport, which currently powers Bombardier's Global 7500
long-range business jet and will power the new Bombardier
Global 8000 aircraft, scheduled for entry into service in 2025.
For the Silvercrest engine, Safran followed on from ground and
flight tests by completing specific high-pressure body tests and
icing tolerance tests under certification conditions. This motor
is now considered sufficiently proven and is available for future
applications.
Mid-thrust engines: The year 2024 saw 50th anniversary
celebrations for CFM International, the 50/50 joint venture
between Safran and GE Aerospace for the CFM56 and LEAP
mid-thrust engines. In 2021, this partnership with GE Aerospace
in CFM International was extended through to 2050.
A total of 1,407 LEAP engines were delivered in 2024, 10% down
on the 1,570 in 2023, as a result of supply-chain difficulties. The
first LEAPs equipped with the new RBS (Reverse Bleed System),
which reduces engine maintenance workload, entered service.
Then to increase the under-wing life of LEAP-1A engines,
particularly in severe and hot environments, a new high-pressure
turbine blade was certified on December 9, 2024 by the civil
aviation authorities in Europe (EASA) and the United States
(FAA). Certification of this new high-pressure turbine blade for
LEAP-1B engines is scheduled for 2025.
Orders for LEAP engines topped 3,000 during the year,
demonstrating the success of this engine with airlines including
easyJet (United Kingdom), Akasa Air (India) and American
Airlines (United States). At the end of 2024, with almost
9,200  LEAP engines delivered since the start of the program,
CFM International’s backlog for the engines stood at more than
11,500 orders (net cumulative orders and commitments) for the
three A320neo, Boeing 737 MAX and C919 programs.

A total of 60 CFM56 engines were delivered in 2024 (up from 52
in 2023). There is still a pipeline of CFM56 engines to be
delivered over the next few years, both to power the military
version of the Boeing 737 (P8-Poseidon) and to provide the spare
engines needed to support the in-service fleet.
Deliveries of high-thrust engines, for which Safran provides
subassemblies as program partner, totaled 195 engines in 2024,
up from 190 in 2023. During the year, Safran continued work on
improving the fan casing for the forthcoming GE9X engine, the
only engine to power the new Boeing 777X long-haul jet, and
21 LM6000 gas turbines (for the CF6 engine) were delivered (up
from 16 in 2023).
Spare parts and service contracts for civil engines: buoyed
by strong growth in air traffic, civil aftermarket (in US dollars)(1)
rose by 24.9%. According to Safran's new metrics for these
activities, Services (in US dollars) rose by 38%, driven by LEAP
flight-hour contracts, and Spare Parts (in US dollars) grew by
16.5%, chiefly on CFM56 and high-thrust engines.
CFM International signed new service contracts for LEAP
engines, notably with American Airlines (United States).
Working toward a business model balance between spare parts
sales and service contracts, CFM International is developing an
Open MRO network involving maintenance, repair and overhaul
(MRO) operations conducted by recognized external players,
which have already signed service contracts, with operators
including Akasa Air (India), Lion Air (Indonesia) and Smartwings
(Czech Republic).
For GE90 high-thrust engines, new service contracts were
signed, extended, restructured or renewed in 2024, as with Silk
Way West Airlines (Azerbaijan), Garuda Indonesia (Indonesia),
Cathay Pacific (China), FedEx (United States), and Emirates
(United Arab Emirates).
Industrial operations: Safran has announced the launch of an
investment plan topping €1  billion to develop its global MRO
network addressing growth of the LEAP engine worldwide.
These
investments
will
facilitate
a
capacity
reaching
1,200  workshop visits per year by 2028. They cover the
construction of 120,000 m2 of additional industrial space for
LEAP maintenance and repair, including:
◼a new site in Brussels (Belgium), operational since early 2024;
◼a new site in Hyderabad (India), operational in 2025;
◼a second workshop in Querétaro (Mexico) and construction
of a new test bench, operational in 2026;
◼a new site in Casablanca (Morocco), operational in 2026;
◼extension of the Villaroche and Saint-Quentin-en-Yvelines
maintenance sites (France), in 2025 and 2026 respectively.
(1)
Civil aftermarket (expressed in USD): this non-audited performance indicator comprises spare parts and MRO (Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul) revenue for
all civil aircraft engines for Safran Aircraft Engines and its subsidiaries only, and reflects the Group's performance in civil aircraft engines aftermarket.

SAFRAN    2024 UNIVERSAL REGISTRATION DOCUMENT

## Page 35

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses
The investment plan also includes development of the worldwide
engine parts repair network, with:
◼a new turbine blade repair facility, with construction of a new
foundry site handling turbine blade production in Rennes
(France);
◼expansion of the Querétaro workshop in Mexico;
◼acquisition, finalized in early 2025, of Component Repair
Technologies (CRT), in Mentor (Ohio, United States). CRT is a
worldwide front-line specialist in the repair of large aircraft
engine parts (cases, rotating parts) for the CFM56, LEAP and
high thrust engines.
In 2024, the new BeCOVER test center was inaugurated at
Herstal in the province of Liège (Belgium). This 3,000 m2 facility
tests all types of compressors for new-generation civil and
military aircraft engines.
Military aircraft engines
Fighter engines: In 2024, 40 M88 engines for the Rafale were
delivered, compared with 42 in 2023, and Safran was selected by
the French government to produce the M88 engines that will be
powering the 42 future aircraft in the fifth stage of the Rafale
contract (5T). During the year, the final stage of 18 Rafales for
Indonesia came on stream, and Serbia signed a contract for
12 new Rafales.
The R&T phase (Phase 1B) of work on the engine for the
new‑generation fighter (NGF) aircraft continued through 2024,
with
development
and
maturation
for
the
advanced
technologies required for demonstrator flights, along with
consolidation of project architectures. The year also saw
observer status in the SCAF program extended to Belgium,
under an agreement signed with France, Germany and Spain.
Transport aircraft engines: A total of 38 TP400 engines were
delivered in 2024 (compared to 34 in 2023). At the end of the
year, orders and purchase commitments covered 143 engines
for the A400M aircraft from Airbus Defence & Space.
Service contract: a contract was signed for the M88 engines
that will be powering Qatar's fleet of 36 Rafales. This contract
offers the Qatar Armed Forces a wide range of services,
including the supply of spare parts, repair and overhaul
operations, and on-site support to optimize maintenance
operations. On Larzac engine maintenance, an agreement was
signed with the Egypt-based AOI (Arab Organization for
Industrialization), which becomes a worldwide authorized and
licensed company for engine support on the Alpha Jet ground
attack and trainer aircraft. This new activity will be carried out
at  a brand-new engine repair workshop employing 30 skilled
workers in Helwan (Egypt).
To support the TP400 engine, a five-year ESS2(1) contract was
signed between OCCAr(2) and Europrop International (EPI). This
covers in-service support of TP400 engines powering Airbus
A400M aircraft in the six countries participating in the program
(France, Belgium, Germany, Spain, Turkey and the United
Kingdom), and will enable participating countries to increase the
availability of their aircraft, while ultimately reducing their
ownership costs.
Helicopter engines
Safran Helicopter Engines delivered 682 helicopter engines in
2024, up from 588 in 2023, with a rise in maintenance operations.
Light helicopter engines: development of the range continued
during the year, with the start of certification flights for Leonardo's
new AW09 light helicopter (Italy), powered by the 1,000 shp
Safran Arriel 2K engine. The Airbus H145 twin-engine multi-role
helicopter, powered by the Safran Arriel 2E engine, also enjoyed
a number of commercial successes in France (42 helicopters for
renewal of the Gendarmerie and Sécurité Civile fleets), Belgium
(15 helicopters), the United Kingdom (6 helicopters) and Cyprus
(6 helicopters).
Engines for medium-weight helicopters: the Safran Arrano 1A
engine was certified by the Chinese (CAAC)(3), Indian (DGCA)(4),
Australian (CASA)(5) and South Korean (KOCA)(6) authorities,
marking an important step towards entry into service for the
Airbus H160 helicopter in these countries. This new 6-ton
twin‑engine helicopter, powered exclusively by Safran Arrano 1A
engines, is already in service in Europe, the United States,
Japan, Brazil and Saudi Arabia. Operators PHI Aviation LLC
(Petroleum Helicopters International) and Omni Helicopters
(Portugal) have also received H160s, the first to be used for
offshore transport missions.
Engines for heavy-lift helicopters: Hindustan Aeronautics
Limited (HAL) (India) and SAFHAL Helicopter Engines Pvt. Ltd.
(SAFHAL) (India)(7) signed a contract to launch design,
development, manufacture and support of a new-generation
high-power engine, the Aravalli, for India's 13-ton IMRH (Indian
Multi-Role Helicopter) and its onboard DBMRH (Deck-Based
Multi-Role Helicopter) version.
Commercial development of the Aneto-1K engine powering the
Leonardo AW189K helicopter continued:
◼in China, where the Civil Aviation Administration (CAAC)
validated the Type Certificate granted to the engine in 2019
by EASA(8);
◼in Latin America, where the Brazilian authorities (ANAC -
Agência Nacional de Aviação Civil) certified this engine,
enabling the first AW189K to enter service with operator Omni
Helicopters, for utility transport missions in the Amazon and
offshore.

## Page 36

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses 
Another Aneto variant, the Aneto-1A, also begun flight tests on
Airbus Helicopters' RACER (Rapid and Cost-Effective Rotorcraft)
demonstrator, with a view to evaluating Safran's Eco Mode
hybridization system in 2025. This system, designed for
twin‑engine helicopters, involves putting one of the two engines
on standby during cruising flight, optimizing power for more
energy-efficient operation.
On development of a heavy helicopter engine for the next
generation of European military helicopters, Safran and MTU
Aero Engines signed a cooperation agreement to form the
50/50 joint venture EURA (EUropean Military Rotorcraft Engine
Alliance). With EURA, the two companies take a major step
towards technology maturation and joint development of an
engine that will be particularly well suited to the ENGRT
(European Next Generation Rotorcraft Technologies) project,
scheduled to enter service in 2040.
The Airbus H225 helicopter, and its military variant H225M,
powered by the Safran Makila 2 engine, enjoyed significant
commercial success in 2024, with orders from the German
Federal Police (Bundespolizei) for 35 units, the Netherlands
(12 units) and Iraq (12 units).
Helicopter engine services: business development continued
throughout the year, with the signing of numerous support
contracts, both SBH® (Support-By-the-Hour) and GSP (Global
Support Package), as with the US Coast Guard, the German
(Bundeswehr), British (Royal Navy) and Danish (Defence
Acquisition and Logistics Organization) armed forces, along with
Global Turbine Asia - GTA (Malaysia), GDAT (China), DRF
Luftrettung (Germany), ADAC Heliservice (Germany), Bristow
Group (United States) and Japan Aerospace Corporation (Japan).
Industrial operations: Safran Helicopter Engines Germany has
launched construction work on its new plant near Hamburg
(Germany). This new 3,000 m² facility will strengthen the Group's
support, logistics, maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO)
capabilities, to keep pace with helicopter market growth in
Germany, Northern Europe, Eastern Europe and Central Asia.
Launch vehicles
Civil space activities: In 2024, Europe's Ariane 6 heavy launcher
successfully completed its first launch from the Guiana Space
Center in Kourou, French Guiana. The rocket carried a number of
small satellites, demonstrators and scientific experiments for
institutions (ESA and NASA), universities and private companies.
At the end of 2024, Arianespace had close to 30 launch contracts
for Ariane 6, including 18 for Amazon's Kuiper constellation.
The Vega light launcher successfully placed the Sentinel-2C
earth observation satellite for the European Union's Copernicus
program into sun-synchronous orbit. This mission was the last
for the Vega launcher, marking the transition to the Vega C
launcher, which made its return to flight by successfully placing
the Sentinel-1C satellite into sun-synchronous orbit for the
European Union's Copernicus program.
Maiaspace(1) continued development of the new Maia reusable
mini-launcher, based on the technological building blocks of
ArianeGroup's Promotheus engine and Themis reusable lower
stage demonstrator.
Military space activities: Following its qualification flight on
November 18, 2023, the program on the new M51.3 strategic
missile for the oceanic component of France's nuclear deterrent
continued. Neither ArianeGroup nor Safran is responsible for
the provision of nuclear warheads for these ballistic missiles.
On the hypersonic weapons front, ArianeGroup continued work
on the V-MAX hypervelocity glider demonstrator.
For the fourth year running, ArianeGroup with its Helix space
surveillance system took part in the AsterX space threat
simulation exercise organized by the French Space Command.
ArianeGroup has also been selected by the European Union
Space
Programme
Agency
(EUSPA(2))
to
contribute
to
development of a space surveillance database for the EU SST
(Space Surveillance and Tracking) program(3).
On December 19, 2024, Safran completed the sale of its stake in
the Roxel joint venture to the missile manufacturer MBDA.
Previously 50/50 owned by Safran and MBDA, Roxel is now a
fully-owned subsidiary of the European missile manufacturer.
(1)
ArianeGroup subsidiary.
(2)
EUSPA – European Union Agency for the Space Programme.
(3)
EU SST is a network of ground-based and space-based sensors capable of surveying and tracking space objects around the earth. It also includes processing
capabilities which provide data on these objects.

1.2.2
Equipment & Defense
Safran is one of the world’s leading suppliers of safety and other critical-function equipment in aerospace and defense applications:
landing, orientation and braking systems; nacelles; electrical systems; and engineering solutions. Safran’s Aerosystems division also
offers safety systems (slides, oxygen masks, onboard oxygen generation, etc.) and fluid management systems. In the Defense sector,
Safran offers a diversified portfolio of products adapted to battlefield transformation: optronics, avionics, navigation, tactical drones,
electronics and mission-critical software. In 2024, the Group acquired Preligens, now Safran.AI, to open up new fields of application for
artificial Intelligence in aerospace and defense.

## Page 38

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses 
1.2.2.1
Landing and braking systems
Safran is a preferred partner of airframers, holding technological
expertise across a large number of sectors and capable of
offering a comprehensive range of products and services.
Safran is notably the sole comprehensive "ATA 32" supplier(1)
(landing gear + brakes + systems).
This business comprises three main product lines: landing gear,
wheels and brakes, and landing and braking systems. Safran
covers these product lines through Safran Landing Systems,
which enjoys a leading position worldwide, equipping some
34,000 aircraft worldwide.
Landing gear
Key characteristics of the business sector
Technology requirements are high, as landing gear must bear
extreme loads during maneuvers on the ground: it must be light,
compact and robust, while being fully adapted to and optimized
for the aircraft’s profile. The business model includes a long
development period - which starts with the initial aircraft
development phase - limited production volumes and regular
service flows.
The market for commercial aircraft landing gear is mainly shared
by
Safran,
Collins
Aerospace,
Liebherr
(Switzerland),
Héroux‑Devtek (Canada) and a handful of niche players
positioned
in
specific
market
segments
(business
jets,
helicopters, drones, etc.).
Group products and programs
Safran is world number-one(2) in landing gear, through Safran
Landing Systems.
The Group designs, manufactures and provides aftermarket
support for landing gear for both civil and military planes and
helicopters of all sizes. It also handles system integration if
requested by the customer, and provides technical assistance,
spare parts and repair services.
Wheels and brakes
Key characteristics of the business sector
The market for wheels and brakes for civil and military aircraft is
currently split among four major global players: Safran, Collins
Aerospace, Honeywell and Meggitt (United Kingdom).
Group products and programs
Safran designs and manufactures wheels and carbon brakes for
civil and military aircraft, and provides related aftermarket support.
Safran is world number-one(2) in this sector for commercial
aircraft of over 100 seats, equipping some 12,600 aircraft with
Safran wheels and carbon brakes. Recurring revenue is
substantial here, since it correlates directly with the size and
lifespan of the fleet in service (specifically, the number of
landings made by aircraft fitted with these systems).
Safran is also involved in electronic and electrohydraulic
systems for aircraft braking and tire/brake/landing gear
monitoring systems.
Safran wheels and carbon brakes also equip more than
1,500 military aircraft and 1,200 business aircraft.
Landing, orientation and braking control systems
Key characteristics of the business sector
This market includes braking systems, front-wheel orientation
systems, landing gear extension/retraction systems, and
monitoring systems. The main participants are Safran, Collins
Aerospace, Crane Aerospace & Electronics (United States),
Meggitt and Liebherr.
Group products and programs
Safran’s operations in the landing and braking system segment
mainly involve Safran Landing Systems. In electronic control
systems for landing gear and brakes, Safran builds on two major
strengths:
◼technological leadership and expertise in complex hydraulic
and electrical systems;
◼ability to offer customers a complete package, including landing
gear, wheels and brakes, and electronic control systems.
Services for landing gear, wheels and brakes and
related systems
Safran provides maintenance services for its own products, and
for competitor products on commercial aircraft with more than
100 seats, and, alone or in partnership, for regional jets and
business jets.
Through its LandingLife® services, Safran delivers tailored
support 24/7 everywhere around the world. The LandingLife
customer portal boasts cost-efficiency and up-to-the-minute
innovation across a range of services, with predictive
maintenance functions harnessing advanced digitalization.
To provide maintenance services for landing gear and landing
and braking systems, Safran has developed international repair
centers in Mexico, the United Kingdom, France, Singapore, the
United States and China. Some of these repair centers are
managed as joint ventures with partners such as Singapore
Airlines Engineering Company, China Eastern Airlines and
Dassault Falcon Jet (United States).
(1)
The Air Transport Association of America (ATA) categorizes aircraft systems into chapters.
(2)

## Page 39

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses
1.2.2.2
Engine systems and equipment
Nacelles and thrust reversers
Key characteristics of the business sector
The nacelle serves as the interface between the engine and the
aircraft. This complex structure is critical to the aircraft’s
performance, fulfilling multiple functions under harsh conditions
(extreme temperatures, lightning protection, dimensional
constraints) and must be kept as light as possible. The nacelle
protects the engine and channels its airflow. It contributes to
aircraft braking during the landing phase (thrust reversers) and
helps reduce noise. It must also ensure ready access to the
engine for maintenance.
Today’s market for aircraft engine nacelles splits into two main
segments, addressed by:
◼nacelle component manufacturers;
◼nacelle integrators such as Safran, capable of supplying
engine manufacturers and airframers with complete nacelles,
and providing aftermarket services throughout the nacelle’s
life cycle.
Safran’s main competitor on the integrated nacelles market is
Collins Aerospace.
Group products and programs
Safran specifies, designs, manufactures and provides aftermarket
support for aircraft engine nacelles through Safran Nacelles. On
this market, the Group ranks second worldwide, with a market
share(1) approaching 25%.
In the segment of nacelles for commercial aircraft with more
than 100 seats, Safran enjoys a long-standing position as a
manufacturer of complete nacelle systems and large thrust
reversers. Safran also developed the COMAC C919 nacelle,
through Nexcelle, a joint venture with Middle River Aerostructure
Systems (MRAS) (United States)(2). Safran also supplies nacelles
for regional jets and is a leading supplier of nacelles for business
jets(3), with a market share of 30%.
Services for nacelles and thrust reversers
Safran offers its customers closely tailored solutions through its
NacelleLife™ range of nacelle maintenance services. NacelleLife
provides operators with maintenance services closely matched
to their specific requirements, from preparation for entry into
service and major maintenance visits, through to removal from
or return to service. NacelleLife solutions generate revenues
from sales of parts, maintenance programs, spares and related
services. NacelleLife also draws on innovative solutions such as
predictive maintenance.
1.2.2.3
Electrical systems and high-tech engineering
The shift toward more electric aircraft systems is a major and
irreversible structural change undertaken by airframers to
increase safety, reduce greenhouse gas emissions, optimize
weight and volume, lower costs and simplify aircraft manufacture,
assembly and maintenance(4).
Three recent market trends afford Safran scope for development:
◼the "more electric" aircraft, as most of the hydraulic and
pneumatic systems found in present-day aircraft are phased
out by electrical systems;
◼electric hybridization, with combustion engines or gas
turbines backed by electric motors to reduce the aircraft’s
carbon footprint;
◼new aircraft projects, for short-distance passenger or freight
transportation, to ease urban mobility, or for general aviation
electrification. These platforms use hybrid or all-electric
propulsion, opening a fresh market for the engines and
electric power management systems in which Safran has
developed expert capabilities.
Electric propulsion, generation and distribution
systems
Key characteristics of the business sector
The electrical systems market mostly covers power generation
and distribution functions. This market is currently commanded
by major participants including Safran, Collins Aerospace,
GE  Aerospace, Honeywell and Transdigm (United States). Only
Collins Aerospace and Safran field a comprehensive offering
spanning main and backup power generation, conversion, and
primary and secondary distribution.
(1)
Source: Safran.
(2)
MRAS is a company of the Singapore-based group ST Engineering.
(3)
Source: Safran.
(4)
See section 1.3.1.1, Electric technologies and new power configurations.
2024 UNIVERSAL REGISTRATION DOCUMENT    SAFRAN
37

## Page 40

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses 
The power conversion market comprises a variety of activities:
◼power load conversion, to meet the needs arising from the
electrification of certain aircraft functions, such as electric
thrust reversers, electric brakes, power load management,
etc.; and
◼mains conversion, to switch from direct current to alternating
current, or to change the voltage.
This critical technological building block was acquired by Safran
with the purchase of Thales’ aeronautical electrical systems
business in 2023.
With recent developments, onboard electrical systems are
also 
extending
to
propulsion
functions.
With
electric
micro‑hybridization or internal hybridization, a conventional
engine can work together with an electric motor or electric
generator, the  two relaying each other or running in tandem
depending on flight phase or mission requirements. This
reduces aircraft fuel  consumption and improves operability of
the conventional engine, relieved of duty during low-speed
phases. Electric hybridization can be combined with the primary
energy source: for example, a turbogenerator (gas turbine
coupled to generators) provides enough electricity to power
several electric motors for aircraft propulsion. For general
aviation and certain light aircraft (VTOL(1)), 100% battery-
produced electric propulsion is possible, though the battery
mass density reduces the operational range of these aircraft to
a few hundred kilometers.
The main players on these new electric propulsion and
hybridization markets are Safran, GE Aerospace, Collins
Aerospace and Honeywell, along with a number of startups,
such as magniX (United States), Evolito (United Kingdom) and
MAGIcALL (United States).
Group products and programs
Safran
covers
the
electric
propulsion,
generation
and
distribution systems sector through Safran Electrical & Power.
Safran stands as a world leader in primary electrical power
distribution on board aircraft, offering customers a wide range
of variable-frequency generators that meet the needs of
helicopter and civil and military aircraft programs. Many aircraft
are fitted with Safran DC engine starter generators, APUs(2) and
backup power systems (Ram Air Turbine [RAT]).
Safran has also developed a new range of electric motors and
generators. Known respectively as ENGINeUS and GENeUS,
these products offer superior performance and high output,
thanks to the integration of power electronics, which increases
power density. They are especially well placed to meet
customers’ needs in hybrid and electric propulsion solutions on
new markets.
Electrical interconnection systems (wiring)
Key characteristics of the business sector
The Group is a leading worldwide supplier(3) of electrical
interconnection systems, which mainly comprise electrical
harnesses for power and data transmission.
Aircraft manufacturers often carry out part of the corresponding
activities in-house. Safran’s main rivals on the aircraft wiring
market are GKN (United Kingdom) and Latécoère.
Group products and programs
Safran’s operations in the wiring and electrical interconnection
system segment involve Safran Electrical & Power.
Safran’s customers in this field include the main airframers and
helicopter manufacturers. It also provides electrical and layout
design work, as well as harness production and installation
support services. If requested, as on upgrade programs, Safran
can take on the planning and onboard installation stages. This
can extend to an end-to-end service covering engineering,
manufacture and installation support. The Group supplies the
harnesses mounted on CFM56 and LEAP engines, and on
landing gear for commercial and business jets and missiles.
Safran also takes charge of fitting electric bays, enclosures and
cabinets for commercial aircraft and helicopters.
Electrical components
Key characteristics of the business sector
Customers in the market for electrical connections in harsh
environments judge suppliers largely on their degree of
qualification and their ability to deliver complete systems and
subassemblies. A determining factor is the capacity for offering
customers expertise across all the components integrated in
these systems and subassemblies.
Competencies in this field are chiefly found with Safran, 3P
Produits Plastiques Performants, Glenair (United States),
Transdigm, Amphenol (United States), Federal Mogul (United
States), TE Connectivity (United States) and Hellermann Tyton
(United Kingdom).
Group products and programs
Through its Safran Electrical Components subsidiary, Safran
holds qualifications in design, production and aftermarket
support for electrical interconnection components, power
contacts and fluid transfer components and subassemblies.
Safran’s components and subassemblies address the defense,
automotive and industry sectors, as well as aviation.
(1)
Vertical Take-Off and Landing aircraft.
(2)
Auxiliary power unit.
(3)

## Page 41

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses
High-tech engineering
Key characteristics of the business sector
Engineering services providers must be able to accompany their
customers on an international market, to offer offshore
capabilities (with a pool of engineers located in lower-cost
countries such as India), to take responsibility for completing
engineering work, to support process improvement and to
develop and retain skills.
The Group’s main competitors are leading French full-service
engineering groups such as Alten, Capgemini Engineering,
Akkodis, and French niche aviation engineering suppliers
including Expleo and Aeroconseil.
Group products and programs
Safran is present in the engineering market through Safran
Engineering Services. In Europe, North America, Asia and North
Africa, Safran provides engineering services primarily on the
aviation market (for airframers and equipment manufacturers),
but also on the rail market, where the Group offers expertise in
electrical systems.
Safran Engineering Services’ expertise in six broad areas
(electrical
engineering,
mechanical
engineering,
systems
engineering, software development, production release support
and customer/publication technical support) is used by various
Group
companies,
chief
among
which
rank
Safran  Electrical  &  Power, Safran Aircraft Engines and
Safran Electronics & Defense. Services are also provided to major
aerospace customers (Airbus, Boeing, Dassault Aviation,
ArianeGroup) and land transport companies (Alstom).
Safran Engineering Services also works toward fulfilling Group
ambitions by providing expertise in digitalization, project
management, additive manufacturing, industrialization and
supply chain management. In this way, it supports the Group’s
transformation,
while
addressing
the
sovereignty
and
confidentiality issues inherent in certain programs.
1.2.2.4
Aerosystems
Key characteristics of the business sector
Most of the high-tech equipment and systems that ensure the
in-flight and on-ground safety, fuel management and on-board
fluid management of airplanes and helicopters are specified
and selected by the airframer at the onset of each new aircraft
program.
They are covered by airworthiness type certification granted by
authorities such as the European Aviation Safety Agency (EASA)
in Europe or the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) in the
United States. This entails substantial investments at the onset
of a program, offset by regular original equipment and spare parts
revenues throughout the program lifespan. However, some
systems are optional, such as helicopter emergency buoyancy
systems, while others, such as life jackets, are selected directly
by operators.
Safran’s main competitors in these businesses in France and
abroad include: Collins Aerospace, EAM Worldwide (United
States), DART Aerospace (United States), Honeywell, Parker
Hannifin Corporation (United States), Eaton Corporation (United
States), Woodward (United States) and Senior Aerospace SSP
(United States).
Group products and programs
Safran Aerosystems designs and manufactures high-technology
solutions to enhance aircraft performance and improve aircraft
and passenger safety in-flight and on the ground. These
systems are installed on most commercial and business aviation
programs, and on most helicopter platforms. They also feature
on several French and European military programs.
Safety and protection systems
Safran’s expertise in safety and protection systems extends to
emergency evacuation systems for civil and military aircraft
(slides, life vests and life rafts), inflatable safety products for
helicopters (safety floats, life vests and life rafts), oxygen
systems and masks for the cockpit and passenger deck, and
protection equipment for military and space missions (survival
suits, vests, anti-G pants, oxygen masks, etc.). With the
acquisition of Air Liquide's aeronautical oxygen and nitrogen
activities in February 2024, Safran also holds expertise in
on‑board oxygen generation systems (OBOGS).
Safran holds world-leading positions(1) in most of these fields.
Aware of the crucial importance of ensuring safety for aircraft
passengers, crew and pilots, Safran offers its customers
engineering, production, technical support and aftermarket
capabilities tailored to their requirements and constraints,
worldwide.
Integrated management and control systems for fluids
and fuel
Safran is one of the world’s leading designers and manufacturers
of hydraulic components and equipment (servo-valves, pressure
switches, etc.), articulated high-pressure, high-temperature
pipe
systems,
and
engine
equipment
(air
valves,
servo‑actuators, etc.).
Backed by its capabilities in fuel circulation, gaging and tank
inerting systems for civil and military programs, Safran is also a
major supplier of fuel management systems. These systems
can also be designed to run on sustainable alternative fuels. In
addition, the Group is proficient in the design, certification and
manufacture of fuel tanks using proprietary mixes and doped
fabrics for light weight and flexibility. These are used primarily in
helicopters, for their crash-resistance properties.
Safran is one of the leading providers of ice detection and
de‑icing systems for aircraft.
Spare parts and services activities
Safran operates an international network specializing in
aftermarket support for customers’ aerospace systems. This
spares and services business primarily involves the sale of spare
parts and the provision of maintenance, repair and overhaul
(MRO) services.
(1)
Ranking criteria: revenue – Source: Safran.
