## Page 42

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses 
1.2.2.5
Electronics and defense
Avionics
Key characteristics of the business sector
Avionics, which play an essential role in the piloting and safety of
civil
and
military
aviation
platforms,
comprise
all
the
mission‑critical onboard electronic systems that support an
aircraft’s operation, navigation and control. This sector is
characterized by long cycles. Its criticality raises crucial
challenges that call for constant innovation in electronics.
Safran responds through ambitious technology roadmaps that
benefit all its markets.
Safran equips all types of aerospace platforms: commercial,
regional, business and military aviation, as well as helicopters,
drones, launch vehicles and satellites. The Group's customers
include leading aircraft and helicopter manufacturers, along
with airlines.
Its main competitors in France and abroad include: GE Aerospace,
Collins Aerospace, Honeywell, Thales, BAE Systems, Liebherr,
Teledyne Controls (United States), Moog (United States) and
Korry Electronics (United States).
Group products and programs
The systems developed by Safran in mission - critical actuation,
avionics, electronics and software contribute to pilot comfort
and efficiency, trajectory control, flight safety, navigation
accuracy and aircraft performance.
Actuation systems
Safran offers electromechanical flight control solutions for all
types of civil and military aviation platforms (planes, helicopters,
drones, etc.), meeting the highest standards of reliability and
safety. From sensors to actuators, Safran solutions improve
aircraft performance and accommodate new, more electric,
architectures.
Safran also provides electromagnetic actuator systems for
other aerospace applications, such as thrust reversers, seats,
and door opening and closing mechanisms.
Data services
Safran covers the whole of the digital chain for airplanes and
helicopters, from automatic data collection to decoding,
extending to in-depth, personalized analysis of flight data.
Cassiopée™, Safran's all-in-one flight data decoding and
analysis solution, now equips more than 150 airlines and many
business jet and helicopter operators worldwide. As well as
providing valuable decision-making assistance for increased
flight safety, this range of services also optimizes operating
costs, maintenance and fuel consumption.
Electronics and critical software
Through Safran Electronics & Defense, the Group designs,
produces and services certified on-board control systems for a
wide range of critical applications, including engine control
systems, thrust reversers, flight and braking controls, landing
gear, and data acquisition and software loading units.
A number of strong, balanced partnerships have been forged,
with FADEC International (joint venture with BAE Systems) and
FADEC Alliance (joint venture between GE Aerospace and
FADEC International) on engine regulation for CFM International
and GE Aerospace engines.
Cockpit and HMI (Human Machine Interface) solutions
Safran offers a range of cockpit solutions for ensuring flight
safety and efficient, comfortable piloting on helicopters as well
as commercial, military and business aircraft:
◼piloting controls: sidesticks, yokes, throttle controls, pedals,
etc.;
◼secondary control levers: shutter controls, air brakes, landing
gears, etc.;
◼control panels: components, retro-lit buttons, integrated
panels, etc.
Safran also develops a full range of LED-based exterior lighting
solutions, along with complete windshield wiper and washer
systems adapted to the specific features of each plane or
helicopter, to guarantee pilots an optimal field of vision,
whatever the weather conditions.
Safran designs and develops, to the highest air safety standards,
emergency locator transmitters (ELTs) for commercial aviation,
general aviation and helicopters, plus personal locator beacons
(PLBs) for the defense market.
Aeronautic navigation
Inertial navigation meets the safety and reliability requirements
of civil and military aircraft, by guaranteeing their positioning and
trajectory. Safran designs and produces inertial sensors, AHRS
(Attitude and Heading Reference System) and inertial navigation
systems. Based on proven, mature technologies such as the
HRG Crystal™ hemispherical gyroscope(1), Safran's SkyNaute is
a highly compact inertial navigation system that delivers
performance and integrity under all circumstances.
Support and services for aerospace customers
Safran’s avionics aftermarket support delivers high value-added
services and solutions tailored to each customer, from
maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO) services to spare parts
sales, equipment delivery and technical support.
(1)
The HRG Crystal gyroscope, a major innovation patented by Safran, is the highest-performance system of its kind on the market. Using this sensor, with its
straightforward mechanism and proven technology, Safran develops new ultra-reliable and extremely compact inertial systems capable of covering a very wide
performance range for civil and military applications in space, air, land and sea environments.

## Page 43

PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses
Safran Electronics & Defense’s MRO centers in France,
the  United States and Singapore provide aftermarket support
for all its aircraft equipment, as well as 24/7 aircraft-on-ground
(AOG) services.
Defense
Key characteristics of the business sector
As a key figure in the sovereignty field, with close to a hundred
years of technological experience, Safran addresses a broad
spectrum of tactical and strategic national security and defense
needs, for observation, decision-support and guidance on land,
sea and air.
Its main customers and partners in this sector are governments
(ministries or armed forces or security forces) and industrial
groups such as Thales, Airbus, ArianeGroup, Dassault Aviation,
MBDA, Naval Group, KNDS(1), Leonardo, BAE Systems, Saab AB
(Sweden), General Dynamics Land Systems (United States),
John Cockerill (Belgium).
Safran’s main competitors for applications in these businesses
in France and abroad include: Thales, Exail Technologies,
Hensoldt (Germany), Meinberg (Germany), BAE Systems,
Spirent (United Kingdom), Leonardo, Elbit Systems (Israel),
L3  Harris Wescam (Canada), FLIR Systems (United States),
Northrop Grumman (United States), Honeywell.
Group products and programs
In the Defense sector, Safran supports its customers by
guaranteeing technological superiority through equipment that
has been tested in theaters of operation, and which helps
protect soldiers and populations. Working from a thorough
understanding
of
day-to-day
challenges,
Safran
applies
high‑performance development methods to drive innovation
that closely addresses emerging issues and the shifting
operational needs of French and international forces.
Safran solutions bring intelligence to programs across the entire
operational chain, for benefits in:
◼observation, for a more accurate appreciation of the tactical
situation;
◼decision, for lighter mental workloads;
◼guidance, for guaranteed trajectories, even in hostile
environments, from the bottom of the ocean to the frontiers
of space.
Safran products and equipment are also beginning to feature
artificial intelligence capabilities, providing forces in the field
with better knowledge of the environment, a lighter cognitive
load, and faster analysis, while ensuring that decision-making
and action remain in the hands of the user.
Airborne combat systems
Safran combines its optronic and inertial expertise to provide
guidance solutions for intelligent air-to-ground weapons
adaptable to all types of airborne missions. An example is the
AASM Hammer(2), a high-precision air-to-ground guidance kit
that can be deployed in all weathers, day and night. AASM
Hammer is currently in service on Rafale fighters belonging to
the French air force and navy as well as in other international air
forces. It comes in several versions and harnesses Safran
expertise in many areas, including infrared and image processing
technology, inertial components with hemispherical resonator
gyro (HRG) and laser guidance. It has proved highly effective in
many overseas operations.
Drawing on its expertise in aeronautical solutions for many
armed forces, Safran also offers airborne solutions tailored to
their needs, such as firing posts, mission preparation, etc.
Air-land combat systems
Air-land combat involves coordinating air and ground forces to
maximize effectiveness on the battlefield. The main challenges
here include air control, particularly as regards observation,
precision ground strikes, and troop protection, while minimizing
losses and collateral damage. The technologies developed by
Safran play a crucial role in the success of these operations.
Discreet surveillance of theaters of operation and precise
geolocation of targets are key success factors for land-based
missions. Safran offers a complete range of high-performance,
modular, combat-proven airborne solutions.
In the area of vehicle and aircraft autonomy, Safran fields
advanced expertise in critical functional chains: navigation and
control, image chain, data transmission, artificial intelligence.
Its Patroller drone system can conduct missions ranging from
ground support to maritime and border surveillance. Already
selected as the French Armed Forces’ tactical drone system,
the Patroller won its first export contract in 2023 with the Greek
Armed Forces, further confirming Safran’s position as a major
European player in tactical drones.
Battlefield digitization is transforming military operations: Safran
develops advanced technologies to improve situational
awareness, management and coordination of the various
players in the field, particularly in terms of fire computers,
cyber‑defense and protection/resilience against the risks of
disruption
to
communication,
navigation
and
command
systems.
Among Safran's latest technologies, the Skyjacker system
replaces navigation satellite signals with modified signals to
divert drone threats with false position information. Skyjacker is
particularly well suited to countering saturating attacks such as
drone swarms.
Onboard optronic equipment
In airborne optronics, Safran participates in major combat
helicopter programs. The STRIX and OSIRIS sighting systems
meet requirements on exceptionally demanding operational
conditions and guarantee the performance of the weapons
systems. Euroflir™ gyrostabilized electro-optical systems provide
valuable service in long-range observation and target location.
They also feature on new airborne intelligence systems: drones,
helicopters and aerostats.
Safran equipment provides protection for land combat vehicles
(tanks, infantry vehicles and light vehicles). Stabilized sighting is
a key factor in ambulant firing capability under day or night
conditions, from target identification to engagement. Safran
equips close to 10,000 land vehicles in France and other countries
with its range of land vehicle sights, including PASEO.
(1)
KNDS (KMW+Nexter Defense Systems) is the merger of Krauss-Maffei Wegmann (Germany) and Nexter (France).
(2)
Armement air-sol modulaire (modular air-to-ground weapon).

## Page 44

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses 
In the naval field, Safran offers a range of optronic surveillance,
fire direction and self-protection systems for surface vessels,
and has recognized expertise in optronic masts and periscopes
for conventional (SSK(1)) and nuclear (SSN(2) and SSBN(3))
submarines.
Soldier equipment, portable optronics and parachutes
Safran offers a complete range of infantry protection, observation
and orientation systems, along with parachutes to enhance
operational efficiency and safety. Flagship products include
solutions using the innovative, modular NeoFelis system (a
modernized version of the FELIN system(4)).
Safran is also one of the world’s leading manufacturers of
portable optronic equipment. It offers a full range for observation,
surveillance, detection, pointing, identification and target
designation, for use under day or night conditions. Its
night‑vision
imagers,
such
as
the
JIM
COMPACT™
multifunction infrared binoculars, along with products from its
subsidiaries Vectronix in Switzerland and Safran Defense &
Space Inc. in the United States, are widely used in overseas
operations by French and allied forces and are considered a
global market benchmark.
Safran is also a world leader(5) in military (troop and cargo)
parachute and protection equipment.
Lasers
Founded in 1966, Compagnie industrielle des lasers (CILAS)
develops, designs, produces and markets laser equipment and
optical solutions for military and civil applications. It is
majority‑owned by HMS Laser, itself equally owned by Safran
and MBDA.
Defense customer support
Safran’s customer support for defense equipment involves
such high value-added services as repairs, equipment delivery,
technical support and training, which are designed to meet the
same goal: ensuring that equipment is in full working order at all
times, a strategy primarily reflected in Global Support Package
contracts.
Navigation & Timing
Key characteristics of the business sector
The accuracy of a myriad of civil and military applications
depends entirely on the use of global navigation satellite system
(GNSS) signals. In response, Safran offers solutions that
guarantee the resilience, integrity and accuracy of transmitted
data, even in conditions where the risk of interference, jamming
or decoying is high.
Navigation & Timing’s main competitors are Honeywell,
Microship/Microsemi (United States), Spirent (United Kingdom),
Teledyne (United States), Meinberg (Germany), Thales, Exail
and Accubeat (Israel).
Group products and programs
Safran has developed, partially through external growth, a full
range of positioning, navigation and timing (PNT) solutions
and equipment, which today serve customers in every sector
such as:
◼Defense, with armed forces;
◼Aviation safety;
◼Aeronautics, including aircraft manufacturers, airlines and
airports;
◼Space, including national and international space agencies,
scientific research centers and New Space players.
For time servers in particular, customers include:
◼GAFAM(6), stock exchanges, global financial systems and
datacenters;
◼transport operators (air, land, rail or sea).
With expertise spanning nearly 100 years, Safran is a
long‑standing player in inertial navigation and the key underlying
technologies: mechanical, thermal, laser, fiber optics, resonant
structure, MEMS(7), etc. Safran inertial sensors feature in
compact, high-performance inertial navigation systems that can
be integrated in all circumstances and hybridized with
satellite‑facilitated position, navigation and time (PNT) data.
Safran is currently number three worldwide for military inertial
navigation systems, and number one in Europe(8).
Safran also covers all other areas of resilient PNT:
◼accelerometers, inclinometers, sensors, gyroscopes and IMUs
(Inertial Measurement Units) using high-precision MEMS;
◼atomic clocks, essential for triangulating satellite positioning.
Safran is the world leader in resilient time and atomic
clocks(8);
◼time servers for critical digital networks;
◼simulators for Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS(9))
to test and validate the performance of GNSS receivers and
other systems using these signals;
◼in GNSS signal interference detection and mitigation, Safran’s
expertise ranges from protection strategies for military bases,
government installations and fixed civil critical infrastructures,
to mobile application technologies. This expertise is applied
to detect, mitigate or thwart GNSS signal spoofing and
jamming, whether on land, at sea or in the air.

PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses
Space
Key characteristics of the business sector
Space offers an immense and still largely unexplored potential
for services and applications, in understanding the universe,
communication, navigation, meteorology, climate change
monitoring, security, etc. All are matters of major political,
economic, industrial and cultural importance. Space exploration
missions are becoming more ambitious, with a growing number
of private operators, satellite constellations, defense and
sovereignty activities, in-orbit services around the planet, etc.
In this regard, Safran’s long-standing customers include space
agencies (ESA(1), NASA(2) etc.) and integrators (Thales Alenia
Space, ArianeGroup, Airbus Defence and Space, etc.), as well as
New Space companies like Leaf Space (Italy) and U-Space
(France).
Group products and programs
In the current context of growing development in the space
sector, Safran intends to confirm its position as a key global
equipment supplier, independent of space prime contractors,
offering solutions to all satellite platform manufacturers and
system prime contractors.
The space-related technologies of Safran Data Systems,
Syrlinks, Safran Reosc and Safran Spacecraft Propulsion have
been brought together under a single division. This division
offers all its customers both purpose-designed solutions and a
range of off-the-shelf products with comprehensive support
services. This mission-critical equipment enables customers to
build and operate complex systems to push back the
boundaries
of
space
exploration,
strengthen
national
self‑reliance and protect the planet.
Earth-space instrumentation and communication
Safran Data Systems supplies high-technology equipment and
solutions
for
the
acquisition,
recording,
telemetering,
processing and management of flight test data, as well as for
pilot mission tracking and space communications.
Safran’s telemetry applications are primarily designed for use
during the test, certification, calibration and maintenance
campaigns run by test centers, missile and aircraft manufacturers,
helicopters, civil or military drones and eVTOL craft. Safran’s
main competitors in this business are US-based, such as
Curtiss-Wright in onboard telemetrics.
Safran supplies modular radio-frequency equipment and
solutions, along with ground stations for satellites and
constellations. Safran’s WeTrack™ service is a worldwide
network of RF sensors for space situational awareness (SSA),
whose applications include monitoring geostationary satellites
and detecting their maneuvers.
With its Syrlinks radiocommunications subsidiary, Safran offers
satellite integrators a range of products enabling them to
control their satellites and manage their data from the ground.
High-performance optics
Safran Reosc designs optical instruments for astronomy, space,
lasers and industry. Its lenses, free-form mirrors and other
components and subassemblies are engineered to the most
demanding specifications of international research organizations
and scientific programs.
Safran Reosc’s capabilities and services cover: opto-mechanical
and thin-film engineering; machining and weight-optimization
for glass and vitroceramic substrates; precision polishing for
materials including glass, ceramics and silicon carbide; optic
metrology for surface shape and structure at nanometric
precision and extremely low asperity; thin-film optical deposition;
and clean-room equipment assembly. Its production runs are
usually short, for just a limited number of units or prototypes.
The main customers in this segment are the European Southern
Observatory (ESO) in astronomy, major prime contractors and
space agencies worldwide, institutes and major international
scientific programs. As part of the EU Copernicus program,
Safran Reosc is actively contributing to Earth observation
capabilities, by designing such optical components as mirrors,
dioptric subassemblies and filters. Depending on the application,
Safran’s main competitors are Thales, Hensoldt, AMOS (Belgium),
L3 Harris Technologies and Coherent (United States).
Electric motors and propulsion subsystems for satellites
and space probes
Safran Spacecraft Propulsion develops plasma propulsion,
which is the preferred solution for satellite and space probe
orbit raising and attitude and orbit control. This technology, also
referred to as Hall-effect or stationary plasma propulsion, has a
considerable advantage over traditional chemical propulsion
systems as regards take-off mass.
Artificial intelligence (AI)
Key characteristics of the business sector
For around a decade now, Safran's strategy with regard to
accelerating digital transformation has involved developing
tools, such as artificial intelligence (AI), to extract information
from large quantities of operational and engineering data with
the purpose of improving decision-making.
Through Safran.AI, the Group develops unique expertise in the
design and industrial-scale implementation of deep learning
algorithms to provide the most powerful AI solutions on the
market. Safran.AI's proprietary methodology focuses on
operational needs to facilitate close adaptation to a wide variety
of application and environment scenarios (secure sites, cloud or
embedded), in aerospace, aeronautics, defense and industrial
applications. With its algorithm factory, the only one of its kind in
Europe, Safran.AI industrializes the production capacities of AI
detectors to shorten the research-to-production lead time,
accelerate production through standardized processes and
tools, and guarantee the traceability of design stages.
Safran's AI solutions are built to address user needs as closely
as possible. The means customers (the main one being the
French Ministry of the Armed Forces) along with partner states
in America, Asia, Europe and NATO, and intelligence centers
and manufacturers.
(1)
European Space Agency.
(2)
NASA – National Aeronautics and Space Administration.

Group businesses 
Group products and programs
The Group intends to improve equipment maintenance,
optimize production processes, automate inspections and
quality control, strengthen safety, and develop advanced
autonomous systems.
To boost competitive performance, Safran pursues artificial
intelligence (AI) work in processing information from images,
video streams and acoustic signals, with ambitious plans on:
◼digital product inspection, where AI is applied to image
processing for defect recognition; and
◼generative AI, which enhances the Group's knowledge and
documentation bases, and boosts productivity.
Convergence
across
Safran.AI's
expertise
and
Safran
Electronics & Defense's mobile equipment also offers major
development prospects.
1.2.2.6
2024 highlights
Landing and braking systems
Landing gear: buoyed by growth in airframer deliveries, Safran
delivered 1,071  landing gear systems across all programs (up
from 995  in 2023). This upward trend in Safran landing gear
deliveries remains stilted by capacity constraints in the Group's
supply chain.
Safran renewed contracts to supply landing gear for the
Airbus  A320 and A350-900 programs, and was notified of
contract extension for Dassault Aviation's Rafale program.
On the development front, Safran landing gear for the Airbus
A321XLR aircraft and new iNLG landing gear for ATR aircraft
have been certified. Safran also continued design and
development work on the integrated landing system for
US‑based Bell Textron's FLRAA(1) tilt-rotor aircraft program.
Safran is also actively preparing the first general overhaul
campaigns for the A350, Boeing 787 and A400M.
Wheels and brakes: The Group’s wheels and brakes business
has been boosted by the recovery in air traffic and the increase
in airframer production. New contracts have been signed for
over 700 aircraft, with airlines including Air India (India), Japan
Airlines (Japan), GOL (Brazil), Korean Air (South Korea) and Royal
Jordanian (Jordan).
Development activities also advanced, with:
◼certification for the "neo adapted" brakes on the new
A321XLR aircraft, which entered service in late 2024;
◼certification for new-generation brakes on A350-1000 aircraft;
◼brake qualification for the future Boeing 777X long-haul aircraft.
Landing, orientation and braking control systems: Safran has
obtained certification for the braking system and associated
hydraulic equipment on the Airbus A321XLR program. The Group
continued to develop braking systems and ECUs controlling the
landing gear extension/retraction and nosewheel steering
functions on Dassault Aviation's forthcoming Falcon 10X
business jet, and completed the preliminary design phase for
the braking system on Airbus Defence and Space's Eurodrone,
the future European military reconnaissance drone.
Services for landing gear, wheels and brakes and related
systems: Safran signed an overhaul contract for landing
systems with Air Algeria (Algeria), for seven Airbus A330
aircraft, and a contract extension with easyJet (United Kingdom)
and IAG (Spain) for around sixty A320s. Safran has also been
selected by Jetblue (United States) to overhaul the landing gear
on its fleet of 102 A320 aircraft. Safran will then be overhauling
some thirty Boeing 787s operated by Kenya Airways (Kenya), IAG
and Thai Airways (Thailand), and a similar number of Boeing
737s operated by AeroMexico (Mexico) and Jet2 (United
Kingdom).
Industrial operations: Safran inaugurated a new laboratory,
named Newton, at its Vélizy-Villacoublay site, near Paris,
specializing in the development and qualification testing of its
wheels
and
carbon
brakes.
This
laboratory
boasts
state‑of‑the‑art testing facilities to bolster the Group's ability to
offer customers ever more efficient, durable and competitive
brakes, and to meet the challenges of rising production rates.
Engine systems and equipment
Nacelles and thrust reversers:
Safran deliveries in 2024 included:
◼622 nacelles for the A320neo and 62 nacelles for the
A330neo, respectively 43 and 8 more than in 2023;
◼754 nacelles for business and regional aircraft, 242 more than
in 2023, a figure boosted by deliveries of nacelles for the new
Gulfstream G700 business aircraft program, which entered
service in 2024.
A number of decisive advances were made in 2024 with:
◼entry into service of Safran nacelles for the Pearl 700 engine
on the new Gulfstream G700 long-range jet;
◼delivery in Hamburg of the first Safran production nacelles for
the A321XLR and the 4,000th Safran nacelle for an A320neo
aircraft;
◼delivery of the 330th nacelle for an A330neo aircraft;
◼delivery of the 2,000th Safran thrust reverser for GE Aerospace
CF34-8E engines powering the Embraer 175-E1 regional jet;

FLRAA - Future Long Range Assault Aircraft.

Group businesses
◼acquisition of 3DMF, a Netherlands-based company
specializing in high-energy hydroforming of metal parts, a
process that involves forming a ductile metal part by means
of fluid pressure produced by a detonation. This innovative
manufacturing
process
strengthens
Safran
Nacelles'
development and production capabilities, both for nacelle
and engine parts, including nozzles and air inlet lips, and in the
manufacture of monolithic structural parts from thick plates.
Support and services for nacelles and thrust reversers:
Safran's business here continued to grow with the rollout of
A320neo nacelle repair licenses with the three major global MRO
players(1): ST Engineering (Singapore), Air France Industries KLM
Engineering & Maintenance and Lufthansa Technik (Germany).
The seven maintenance, repair and overhaul sites concerned are
now operational.
Several maintenance service contracts were signed with
SkyWest Airlines (United States), Delta Air Lines (United States),
Republic Airways (United States), and two contracts with
Sunclass Airlines (Denmark), bringing the number of aircraft
under service contracts over the 1,000 threshold.
Recognition of Safran Nacelles' efforts in 2024 came with:
◼seventh place in Airbus' top 50 after-sales suppliers;
◼Best Collaboration Supplier Award at the Rolls-Royce Global
Aerospace Conference in Bristol.
Electrical systems and high-tech engineering
Electric propulsion, generation and distribution systems:
Safran signed a contract with Bell Textron Inc. to supply
starter‑generator systems for the US Army's Future Long-Range
Assault Helicopter (FLRAA) program. This Safran high-voltage
starter-generator system will be capable of producing the power
needed to meet the evolving electrical requirements of military
aircraft. The equipment will be designed, developed and
qualified at Safran's centers of excellence in Twinsburg (Ohio)
and Sarasota (Florida) in the United States.
Safran was also selected by Boeing to supply the electrical
generation system for NASA's (National Aeronautics and Space
Administration) X-66 flight demonstrator. Safran’s electrical
generation system, comprising a variable-frequency generator
and its control electronics, has been adapted for direct
integration on the Pratt & Whitney GTF (Geared Turbo Fan)
engine, and will provide electrical power of over 100 kW.
In early 2025, Safran obtained EASA certification for the
ENGINeUS™ 100, the first electric engine certified to power
new  air mobility. ENGINeUS meets the various needs of the
new  mobility market because it can integrate into multiple
architectures: from 100% electric propulsion for small 2‑ to
4‑passenger aircraft, to distributed hybrid propulsion for
19‑passenger regional jets, right through to electric hybridization
for future generations of 150-passenger commercial aircraft
engines.
Electrical Wiring Interconnection Systems (EWIS): Safran
was selected by French aircraft manufacturer VoltAero to
supply
electrical
wiring
for
the
five-seat
Cassio
330
electric‑hybrid certification aircraft. Safran will be designing and
manufacturing the electrical distribution system and electrical
wiring for the propulsion system (a Safran ENGINeUS 100
electric motor). The wiring, qualified for power ratings up to 800
volts at continuous supply, will be designed to withstand partial
discharge at altitude.
Electrical components: Safran continued to restructure its
electrical components business (Safran Electrical Components),
and took steps to improve competitiveness and control costs, in
particular by streamlining its industrial footprint. R&T efforts
have been focused on innovative technologies that are in line
with the Group’s roadmap (notably high-power connectors for
the electrification of aircraft propulsion, high-temperature
elastomers, and electromagnetic protection for high-thrust
harnesses).
Industrial operations: Safran celebrated the opening of a new
13,500 m2 plant at its Chihuahua campus in Mexico, specializing
in the manufacture of electrical wiring for the Boeing 737,
Boeing 767 and Boeing 777 programs.
Aerosystems
In Safety and Protection Systems, Safran finalized the
acquisition of Air Liquide's oxygen and nitrogen aeronautical
businesses in February 2024. This acquisition brings Safran
expertise in On-Board Oxygen Generating Systems (OBOGS),
and rounds out its product range in equipment designed to
ensure the safety of both crews and passengers on board civil
and military aircraft.
Safran's new Light Weight Emergency Slide (LWES) for the
Airbus A320 program has been certified. This uses a new, lighter
material, bringing the weight of the equipment down while
offering better performance than that of current platform slides.
Safran's efforts on emergency evacuation systems were also
recognized by Chinese aircraft manufacturer COMAC, with a
Production Ramp Up Award for the C909 and C919 programs.
A strategic partnership was signed with Vietjet (Vietnam) to
supply safety equipment for the airline's B737 MAX aircraft. This
partnership covers both the supply by Safran of essential safety
components including liferafts, protective breathing equipment,
portable oxygen cylinders and masks (POCA - Portable Oxygen
Cylinder Assembly), life jackets, and support services to be
provided by Safran Aerosystems Services Asia, based in
Singapore.
In integrated fluid and fuel management and control systems,
Safran was selected by Airbus Defense & Space and Leonardo to
supply the fuel distribution system and the ice detection and
protection system for the future European MALE RPAS (Medium
Altitude Long Endurance Remotely Piloted Aircraft System) drone
(Eurodrone). The Group will be designing, developing, qualifying
and producing this equipment.
(1)
MRO: Maintenance, Repair and Overhaul.

Group businesses 
In industrial operations, Safran announced the construction of
a new aeronautical equipment plant, making evacuation
systems and seat components, in Chihuahua, Mexico. This is
due to open in 2025.
Safran also continued to expand its production capacity in
France, launching the construction of new buildings in:
◼Cognac (Charente), for its rescue buoyancy and rafting
activities;
◼Soignolles-en-Brie
(Seine-et-Marne)
and
Caudebec-lès-
Elbeuf (Seine-Maritime) for fluid and fuel control systems.
Electronics and defense
Avionics
Actuation systems: On July 21, 2023 Safran announced the
contemplated
acquisition
of
Collins
Aerospace’s
high‑technology actuation and flight control activities, which are
mission critical for commercial and military aircraft and
helicopters.
On November 20, 2023, Safran was informed of the Italian
government's decision to exercise its “Golden Power“, thus
opposing the sale to Safran of Microtecnica S.r.l.(1).
The Group subsequently made a number of commitments,
compatible with the targeted objectives of this acquisition,
which address the concerns expressed by the Italian
government and provide adequate safeguards of Italian national
interests. On June 6, 2024, Safran was then informed by the
Italian government of its decision ultimately to approve the sale
to Safran of Microtecnica S.r.l.
In the United Kingdom, the Secretary of State reviewed the
notification made under the National Security and Investment
Act and informed Safran that he will take no further action in
relation to the proposed acquisition. This constitutes an
unconditional clearance.
In the United States, the Committee on Foreign Investment in
the United States (CFIUS) approved the proposed acquisition on
January 16, 2025.
To obtain the necessary merger control approvals, Safran has
initiated the sale of its electromechanical actuation business, as
described in the section below.
Completion of this proposed acquisition therefore remains
subject to obtaining the necessary merger control approvals,
notably conditional on the approval and completion of the sale
of the electromechanical actuation business.
On December 20, 2024, Safran announced that it had signed a
definitive agreement to sell to Woodward its electromechanical
actuation business in the United States, Mexico and Canada.
This transaction includes intellectual property, operations assets,
talent and long-term customer agreements for Horizontal
Stabilizer Trim Actuation (HSTA) systems(2) for aircraft stabilization
to support safe and efficient flight.
The sale will also include other electromechanical products and
electronic control units with a portfolio of commercial airliner
and business aircraft applications.
The transaction marks another important milestone towards the
acquisition by Safran of Collins Aerospace's actuation and flight
control activities.
It is expected to close sometime in mid-2025, once all
customary terms and conditions of the agreement are met and
regulatory requirements are fulfilled, subject to obtaining the
merger control approvals enabling Safran to complete its
acquisition of Collins Aerospace's flight control and actuation
businesses.
Data services: several contracts for the Cassiopée™ flight data
analysis service were signed, as with the Korea Institute of
Aviation Safety Technology (KIAST) and the airlines China
Southern Airlines (China), Air China Inner Mongolia (China),
Canaryfly (Spain), Condor (Germany), Indigo (India), Uni Air
(Taiwan), Tassili Airlines (Algeria) and Gulf Air (Bahrain).
Electronics and critical software: The Safran subsidiary
Asterios Technologies announced the release of a new version
of its Asterios software suite, for developing real-time
mission‑critical
software
applications
for
systems
with
multicore electronic boards. Asterios addresses software
development needs in fields where safety and reliability are
prime concerns, such as aeronautics, new airborne mobility
systems, drones, space, defense, nuclear power, etc. For
example, Safran uses Asterios in systems as diverse as a secure
flight control system, the Skynaute inertial unit, and the new
control system for the M88 engine that powers the Rafale.
Safran Electronics & Defense and the French Civil Aviation
Authority (DGAC) signed a support agreement for the OHPERA
R&T project, which is to demonstrate the technical and
economic feasibility of a processor based on the new Risc-V
Open Hardware architecture for critical civil applications (flight
controls, FADEC, etc.) on future single-aisle aircraft (NSA). Airbus,
Thales and ONERA are also involved in this project.
Cockpit and HMI (Human Machine Interface) solutions:
Safran Electronics & Defense has been selected by US aircraft
manufacturer JetZero to develop pilot controls for its Blended
Wing Body demonstrator aircraft. Safran will be supplying the
throttle, rudder, airbrake and flap controls, and cockpit
components.
Aeronautic navigation: Safran Electronics & Defense has
received an order from US aircraft manufacturer JetZero to
supply SkyNaute inertial navigation systems for its Blended
Wing Body aircraft demonstrator.
At the same time, Safran Electronics & Defense and the French
Civil Aviation Authority (DGAC) have signed a support
agreement for the RESIN (REsilient and Safe Inertial Navigation)
project. This R&T project targets new technologies for the
resilience of navigation systems in civil aeronautics, and the
optimization of development and production processes for
inertial units.
(1)

(2)
Notably used for the Airbus A350.

PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses
Defense
For wider access to major US Department of Defense (DoD)
contracts, the Group founded Safran Defense & Space Inc.,
which operates under US Special Security Agreement (SSA) and
Defense Secret status. Safran Defense & Space Inc. comprises
two of the Group's US companies: Safran Optics 1 and Safran
Data Systems Inc. which already had these approvals. A third
company, Safran Federal Systems, will become part of Safran
Defense & Space Inc. in 2025. Other Group companies are also
likely to join them in time.
Air-land combat systems: following certification in 2023, the
first series-production Patroller tactical UAV system was
delivered to the French army.
Safran, in partnership with Edge Autonomy (United States), has
unveiled its new Lanner vertical take-off and landing (VTOL)
tactical drone. This drone, based on the US company's
"Penguin" platform, was specifically designed to meet the
requirements of the French Armed Forces' SDTL (light tactical
drone system) program. The Lanner drone can carry two loads
simultaneously: electro-optical or infrared systems, electronic
warfare systems (for radio signal detection or jamming, for
example), radar or munitions. This means it can carry out various
types of mission, in intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance,
on land and at sea.
Safran also launched its Skyjacker anti-drone solution, which not
only took part in the protection arrangements for the Paris 2024
Olympic and Paralympic Games, but was also embarked aboard
French Navy frigates deployed in the Red Sea as part of
Operation Aspides. Trials carried out in the spring had already
proved particularly conclusive, confirming Skyjacker's value as
an effective and cost-efficient means of protecting front-line
vessels against drone threats.
Onboard electronic equipment: Safran has been selected to
supply the PASEO Artillery Observation and Designation
advanced panoramic sighting system for integration on Hanwha
Defence Australia's Redback infantry fighting vehicle (IFV). This
advanced solution from Safran's PASEO system provides
long‑range intelligence, surveillance, target acquisition and
reconnaissance capabilities for the Redback IFV supplied by
Hanwha to the Australian Defence Force (ADF) under the LAND
400 Phase 3 project. The contract provides for the production
and delivery of PASEO sights and associated equipment.
The French Directorate General of Weapons Procurement (DGA)
awarded the contract to modernize the sights on the upgraded
Leclerc tank to the joint venture (Groupement Momentané
d'Entreprises - GME) comprising KNDS France and Safran
Electronics & Defense. The tank's “observation and engagement”
functions will benefit from the latest optronic and inertial
technologies, based on Safran's 30 years of experience with the
PASEO sight. The Leclerc tank sight upgrade program covers
over 200 tanks, with production running until 2035.
In marine sights and periscopes, Singapore's Defence Science
and Technology Agency (DSTA) selected Safran to equip its
ships. The contracts signed cover the supply of optronic
observation systems and processing units for GFCS (Gun Fire
Control Systems) on the Singapore Navy's new MultiRole
Combat Vessels (MRCV) and Offshore Patrol Vessels (OPV).
Safran was also chosen by the Egyptian Navy to equip ten
Offshore Patrol Vessels (OPVs) with VIGY4 for optronics and
Argonyx for inertial navigation. These advanced systems from
Safran will significantly enhance Egypt's naval defense
technology. The contract was signed with NVL Egypt, a joint
venture between the Egyptian government and Lürssen, a
German company specializing in the shipbuilding industry.
In March 2024, faced with the growing threat of kamikaze drones
in the Red Sea, the French Navy issued an "urgent operational
requirement" to equip all FREMM and Horizon air defense frigates
with Safran's PASEO XLR marine sights. With their sophisticated
day and night observation capabilities, these provide earlier
threat detection, for advance reaction and decision-making on
the self-defense solution best suited to the situation.
Soldier equipment (portable optronics and parachutes): Safran
has received several major orders from the NSP(1) on behalf of
member countries, for EPC(2) round parachutes and for JIM
Compact and Moskito TI multifunction binoculars, confirming
Safran's leading position in the high-performance multifunction
binocular segment.
Lasers: the HELMA-P anti-aircraft system from CILAS was
successfully deployed for anti-drone protection at the 2024
Olympic and Paralympic Games in the Paris region. The French
Directorate General of Weapons Procurement (DGA) and CILAS
continued the test campaign underway, with the French Navy in
particular.
Navigation & Timing
Safran and Poland's Wojskowe Zakłady Elektroniczne (WZE)
have signed a contract to supply around 200 Geonyx™ inertial
navigation and pointing systems to the Polish Armed Forces for
use in the PILICA+ and NAREW air defense programs. The
contract also includes the transfer of production of these inertial
units to WZE. Safran will produce the main components, and
WZE will assemble and test them before delivering the Geonyx
systems to the Polish Armed Forces.
At the same time, to optimize the field performance of its
Geonyx range of inertial sensors, Safran recently incremented it
with a new NAVWAR option, an algorithm that detects decoy
and jamming threats. This option enhances the reliability of
inertial navigation data.

Group businesses 
For time servers, Safran signed a contract to develop a White
Rabbit system for the UK Research and Innovation (UKRI)
synchrotron
(particle
accelerator)
near
Oxford
(United
Kingdom). White Rabbit will enhance the performance of the
British synchrotron through its ability to synchronize remote
devices in under 5 billionths of a second (5 nanoseconds). This is
the first time this Safran system has been selected for a
scientific facility requiring time-critical precision.
Safran also unveiled NAVPULSE, a new naval data distribution
unit (DDU) designed to centralize navigation data from all
onboard sensors for optimized distribution. The aim is to offer
an optimal navigation solution, meeting the rigorous demands of
modern naval operations by incrementing an ultra-precise time
server with algorithms to detect decoy and jamming attempts,
thereby ensuring uninterrupted operation, data accuracy and
reliability.
Space
Earth-space instrumentation and communication: Safran has
signed a contract with the Swedish Space Corporation (SSC) to
supply an optical ground station capable of communicating at
data rates ten to a hundred times higher than radio-frequency
communications. This new optical ground station, with laser
telescope and optronic systems for optimum sky coverage, will be
built in Chile in 2025. This marks a further milestone for Safran,
which intends to play a leading role in this sector, while
maintaining its leading position in radio-frequency communication
solutions.
In monitoring satellites and detecting satellite maneuvers, the
Safran WeTrack™ global network of radio-frequency sensors
demonstrated its efficacy during the AsterX space threat
military simulation exercise organized by the French Space
Command(1). As of 2019, the French Space Command enshrined
the use of the commercial WeTrack™ service in its strategy by
renewing its satellite data orders for its entire global coverage.
In the field of satellite radio communications, the Safran
subsidiary
Syrlinks
has
been
selected
to
supply
the
new‑generation telemetry, tracking and control transceivers for
Ghalam's forthcoming KazEOSat microsatellite(2), which will be
performing functions in resource monitoring and management,
land use mapping and environmental monitoring.
High-performance optics: The Italian consortium AdOptica
chose Safran to supply the thin blades for the MORFEO
deformable mirrors in one of the first-light instruments for the
Extremely Large Telescope (ELT) currently under construction in
Chile. This further strengthens Safran’s leading position as a
manufacturer of thin blades for the deformable mirrors used in
all terrestrial astronomy telescopes to improve image quality.
Electric motors and propulsion subsystems for satellites and
space probes: Safran has announced the construction of a new
assembly line for electric propulsion systems for small satellites
in Colorado (United States). The aim is to meet growing demand
from the commercial and defense sectors in the area. The
US‑made EPS®X00 system produced here will be supporting
growth in the North American small satellite market. Designed
to meet the specific requirements of the New Space market, it
is extremely versatile and suitable for all types of low-Earth orbit
applications.
Artificial Intelligence
Safran has acquired Preligens, one of the leaders in the
aerospace and defense sector. The company was renamed
Safran.AI. Preligens, founded in 2016, offers proven solutions
based on artificial intelligence (AI) to analyze high-resolution
images, FMV (full motion video) and acoustic signals. The
acquisition enables Safran to accelerate progress on its digital
transformation roadmap, diversifying Safran.AI's AI solutions for
application to Industry 4.0 fields.
At the Eurosatory event, Safran launched its ACE (Advanced
Cognitive Engine) on-board artificial intelligence solution to
improve
knowledge
of
the
environment
and
provide
decision‑making support for forces in the field.

Group businesses
1.2.3
Aircraft Interiors
To ensure passenger safety and optimize comfort, Safran develops cabin interiors, passenger and crew seats, water and waste
management systems, in-flight entertainment systems (RAVE™), and interior retrofit for commercial aircraft.
The aircraft interiors business addresses both airframers and operators. These airframers and operators see aircraft interior design as
a factor in market differentiation and a marker of service level. They therefore demand high standards in both the appearance and the
quality of aircraft cabin equipment.
Whereas most aircraft systems are usually specified by the airframer(1), interior furnishings for commercial aircraft are also specified by
operators(2). In addressing these needs, equipment suppliers also ensure that their products comply with the requirements set by civil
aviation authorities such as the European Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) in Europe or the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) in the
United States.
The Aircraft Interiors business breaks down into two key sectors:
CABINS
50
◼Cabin interiors for commercial, regional and business aircraft
◼Water and waste management systems
◼Ventilation, air conditioning, in-flight entertainment and satellite connectivity systems
◼Spare parts and services for cabin interiors
SEATS
51
◼Seats for passengers, crews and helicopters

## Page 52

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses 
1.2.3.1
Cabins
Cabin interiors for commercial, regional
and business aircraft
Key characteristics of the business sector
The market for aircraft interiors, which cover a large part of what
aircraft passengers see and use, is driven by the cabin design
policies of operators (airlines, leasing companies) and
maintenance centers.
This market is dominated by a few world players, including
airframers themselves, as with Boeing Interiors Responsibility
Center (United States) and aircraft equipment manufacturers
such as Safran, AVIC Cabin Systems (China), Diehl (Germany),
Collins Aerospace, JAMCO (Japan), TCI Aircraft Interiors
(Turkey) and Bucher (Switzerland).
Group products and programs
Safran is a world leader in cabin interiors for commercial and
business aircraft, through Safran Cabin.
Safran designs, certifies and manufactures equipment needed
for fitting and retrofitting cabin equipment and furnishings.
Here, Safran holds extensive expertise in high-tech molding
processes and in advanced composite structures and cabin
panels. Safran also offers customers an integrated equipment
range with galleys, galley inserts, lightweight cargo containers,
lavatory furniture, etc.
Cabins for VIP aircraft
The market for short- and long-haul VIP and Head of State
aircraft is a highly specialized market with low volumes (5 to 20
aircraft per year worldwide) and custom-designed cabins.
Safran, through Greenpoint Technologies, is one of the world’s
leading players in this market, the main competitors in which are
AMAC Aerospace (Switzerland), Lufthansa Technik (Germany),
Jet Aviation (Switzerland) and Comlux (Switzerland).
Water and waste management systems
Key characteristics of the business sector
Water and waste management systems are an integral part of
aircraft development programs. Safran’s main competitors in
this sector are Collins Aerospace and Diehl, which, like Safran,
offer complete systems. A few niche players, such as Franke
Aquarotter (Germany) and Adams Rite Aerospace of the
Transdigm group (United States), specialize in specific
components such as faucets and water heaters.
Group products and programs
Through Safran Cabin, the Group is one of the world’s leading
players in integrated water and waste management systems on
aircraft, fielding expertise in development, certification, and
production and aftersales support. Its solutions enjoy wide
market recognition for innovation and extreme reliability.
Safran has developed cutting-edge technologies such as tanks
in composite material, ultraviolet sterilization of drinking water,
low-pressure pipes in composite material and a range of
ultrasound level sensors. It offers drinking water management
and distribution systems for different types of aircraft (commercial,
business and military). The Group developed the first chemical
lavatories and was one of the pioneers in vacuum-flush
lavatories on planes. Its capabilities in this sector today extend
to integrated waste and water management and drinking water
distribution systems.
Ventilation and environment control systems
Key characteristics of the business sector
Air conditioning systems vary with market segment:
◼in commercial and some business aircraft, they are powered
by the auxiliary power unit (APU), meaning cabin air is
constantly refreshed with air from outside;
◼in aircraft without an APU, such as small private aircraft and
helicopters, air conditioning systems use closed-loop
circulation.
The main players in the commercial aircraft air conditioning
market are Collins Aerospace, Honeywell and Liebherr, which
offer integrated systems covering air conditioning, bleed air and
de-icing systems. There are several key players in other market
segments, including Enviro Systems (United States), Secan
(France), Fimac (Italy) and Air Comm (United States).
Group products and programs
For airframer and system builder customers, Safran is present in
the APU-equipped aircraft segment through Safran Ventilation
Systems, which designs, qualifies, produces and markets
ventilation
equipment
(low-pressure
valves,
flaps,
fans,
electrical and mechanical, filters, etc.) for cooling the cabin and
avionics systems on commercial, business and military aircraft.
Safran also provides a specific brake cooling system for all
Airbus airframes.
A world leader in ventilation equipment for civil aviation, Safran
Ventilation Systems has a presence with all major aircraft
manufacturers. Safran also provides maintenance services
directly to operators through a network of repair centers in
Europe, the United States and Asia.
50
SAFRAN    2024 UNIVERSAL REGISTRATION DOCUMENT

## Page 53

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses
In-flight entertainment and satellite connectivity
solutions
Key characteristics of the business sector
With air passenger volumes on the rise, and intensifying market
competition, in-flight entertainment (IFE) offered on commercial
aircraft is becoming an increasingly important customer appeal
factor for airline companies. Through Safran Passenger Innovations,
Safran has developed entertainment and connectivity systems
that enhance aircraft passengers’ well-being on board. The
programs on this kind of fit-out usually have short development
cycles, and Safran stands out from the competition in this
segment due to its ability to offer complete systems.
Safran’s main competitors in this market are Panasonic (Japan)
and Thales.
Group products and programs
Safran offers a variety of innovative in-flight entertainment (IFE) and
connectivity systems for commercial aircraft.
RAVE (Reliable, Affordable and Very Easy) is an innovative,
stand-alone IFE system whose media content is displayed on
the seat screen, which simplifies the onboard network and gives
passengers easy access to on-demand audio and video. As well
as offering passengers a content and activities catalog, Safran’s
RAVE system also includes a connectivity solution, with a WiFi
connection for broadband internet access. RAVE also offers
lightweight screens with low electricity consumption, benefiting
from a partnership with a major player in consumer electronics
offering the latest technological advances in this field. With its
uncomplicated design, RAVE also offers simplified maintenance
and low cost of ownership.
Safran is also involved in secure, reliable global satellite
connection systems through Safran-certified terminals fitted
on  Boeing (RAVE AeroConnect) and Airbus (Airspace
LinkHBCplus) airliners.
Spare parts and services for cabin interiors
Safran provides maintenance and retrofit support for aircraft
cabin interiors through a number of sites certified by civil
aviation authorities such as FAA and EASA. Customers also
have access to several authorized Group locations offering rapid
fitting and repair, giving Safran the capability to upgrade and
reconfigure any aircraft interior in accordance with operator
requirements and applicable regulations.
1.2.3.2
Seats
Aircraft seats
Key characteristics of the business sector
Aircraft seats have to meet demanding requirements in terms
of appearance and quality, because airline companies consider
them an important brand image factor enabling them to stand
out from the competition in terms of service quality. The
equipment supplier adapts seats to the specific needs expressed
by each individual airline company, which means production
runs tend to be smaller and diversity wider. To reduce aircraft
fuel consumption, seat weight is a determining factor.
This market is determined by growth in the air transportation
market and by airline companies’ cabin interior policies.
In addition to Safran, the other major players in aircraft seats are
Collins Aerospace, Recaro (Germany) and Adient Aerospace
(United States).
Group products and programs
Through Safran Seats, Safran is one of the world leaders in
aircraft seats, with over one million seats installed on
commercial aircraft flown by more than 150 airlines. Safran has a
26% market share in aircraft seats.
Safran expertise covers the whole seat manufacturing chain,
from design and certification through to assembly. This expertise
is applied to offer customers a full range of innovative and
customized seats combining ergonomics, comfort, aesthetics
and cabin space optimization, in terms of passenger and
storage capacity, etc.
Passenger seats
Safran offers its customers a full range of passenger seats
(Economy, Premium Economy, Business and First Class) for
fitting on a wide range of aircraft. The seats address specific
airline company needs on passenger comfort and satisfaction,
and what can be tight operating constraints on efficiency
optimization.
With
its
wide
range
of
seats
and
ability
to
offer
made‑to‑measure solutions, Safran meets the needs of
individual airline companies of all business models and aircraft
user profiles (low-cost through to five-star, regional operators,
etc.).
Technical seats
Safran’s expertise in technical seats for flight-deck and cockpit
personnel covers all the features, technologies and materials
needed for developing products that combine safety, quality and
innovation.
Helicopter seats
Safran products meet the specific needs of the highly demanding
market for seats for vertical take-off aircraft, integrating
functions such as an energy absorption system for enhanced
pilot and passenger safety along with innovative seat actuator
systems and comfort functions.

## Page 54

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses 
Spare parts and services for seats
Safran provides worldwide sales and technical support
throughout the service life of its seat products, meeting
customer demand even in the most critical situations.
Safran services also extend to cabin retrofits. Given the service
life of aircraft seats (five to fifteen years), cabins will need
retrofitting two or three times during the aircraft’s career.
As  well as supplying the new seats, Safran also provides
the  expertise needed for certification of the new cabin
configurations.
Safran also offers solutions meeting specific operator needs in
personnel training, flight-per-hour support, reliability and
maintenance analysis, annual orders and advance stocks.
1.2.3.3
2024 highlights
Cabins
Buoyed by recovery in the long-haul market, Safran's Cabin
business saw strong demand from airlines for retrofits and a rise
in service activities (mainly spare parts). Profitability was largely
driven by service business, in galleys and water and waste
management equipment, etc., and by the benefits of previous
reorganizations.
Additionally, Safran Passenger Innovations made a positive
contribution to recurring operating income, largely due to
in‑flight entertainment products.
Cabin interiors: the Group achieved a number of commercial
successes, including:
◼in North America:
●Safran SpaceFlex equipment was chosen by two airlines to
equip 59 aircraft in the A320 family. By combining galleys
and lavatory units, SpaceFlex enables airlines to increase
the number of seats in the aircraft,
●the Safran NUVO range of electrical inserts was selected
by two airlines to equip 26 A321XLR aircraft and
39 Boeing 787 aircraft;
◼in the Middle East:
●an operator decided to retrofit its 15 A380 and
75 Boeing 777 aircraft with Safran's top-line business class
units;
◼in Europe:
●Safran's lavatory units were chosen for the refurbishment
of 20 A350 aircraft,
●Safran received orders for cabin refit equipment on
Lufthansa (Germany) A350 aircraft.
Several lessors selected Safran cabin equipment, including:
◼in the Middle East, Safran galleys for 42 Boeing 737 MAX
aircraft; and
◼in Asia, crew rest areas and galleys for five Avolon (Ireland)
A330neo aircraft.
Water and waste management on board aircraft: Safran’s
CUBE galley waste disposal system won a Crystal Cabin Award(1)
in the "Cabin systems" category. This system consumes 30%
less water than a conventional galley waste disposal unit and
features a UV light system to help suppress odors and prevent
bacterial growth. The CUBE system also facilitates operations
for the crew by freeing up space on the work surface.
In-flight entertainment solutions: Safran has unveiled the
compatibility
of
certain
versions
of
its
RAVE
in-flight
entertainment system with extended reality equipment. This
gives passengers access to an immersive cinematic experience,
and enables airlines to differentiate themselves through the
entertainment system while deploying a range of digital solutions.
Safran also launched its RAVE Cloud Services state-of-the-art
cloud platform for in-flight entertainment and connectivity
(IFEC). This offers distribution services and Edge Cloud
Computing services for fast, remote updates of IT data via
satellite, cellular and WiFi networks.
Satellite connectivity: Safran's satellite link equipment for
commercial aircraft went into service on Emirates' and Ethiopian
Airlines' new A350 fleets, and scored several major commercial
successes, as with Thai Airways (Thailand), Turkish Airlines
(Turkey) and Air Mauritius (Mauritius).
(1)
Held annually in Hamburg, Germany, the Crystal Cabin Award is the international award for excellence in aircraft interior innovation.

PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Group businesses
Seats
Growth in the Seats business stems mainly from a sharp
increase in business-class seat deliveries (2,482 units in 2024, up
from 983 in 2023). Profitability here improved significantly in
2024, reaching operating breakeven through both services and
original equipment volumes. Ongoing efforts in the industrialization
and engineering processes are bearing fruit.
In economy and premium economy passenger seats, new
orders have been signed with customers including:
◼in the Middle East:
●in the United Arab Emirates, an airline chose to retrofit
94  wide-body aircraft (Boeing 777X and A380) with
30,000 Safran economy-class seats,
●in Saudi Arabia, following a major order for Safran
business-class seats in 2023, the Safran Z200 seat was
selected by an airline to equip 60 new A320neo aircraft, and
●91 wide-body aircraft (Boeing 777-300, Boeing 787 and
A330) will be equipped with 2,000 Safran premium
economy Z535i seats;
◼in Asia:
●the Safran premium economy Z535i seat was chosen by a
Taiwanese airline for five Boeing 787-9s, 18 A350s and
14 Boeing 777s,
●a South Korean airline ordered 2,672 economy-class seats,
and
●in Thailand, an order was signed for 1,450 Safran
premium economy seats for 44 aircraft;
◼in Europe, an airline completed its order for Safran Z110i
Economy Class seats to equip its A220 aircraft.
In business and first-class seats, new orders included the
following in Asia:
◼in Taiwan, an airline will be the launch customer for the Safran
Versa seat, which offers the comfort of a spacious, fully flat
bed, for its future 18 A350 aircraft;
◼the Skylounge Core seat has been selected to equip three
A330 aircraft;
◼in Japan, the Z600 seat was chosen to equip 21 Boeing 737
MAX aircraft as original equipment, and another airline chose
the Z600 seat to retrofit six Boeing 767 aircraft.
The Euphony wireless system co-developed by Safran and the
French company Devialet features loudspeakers on each side of
the headrest, which adapt in real-time to the audio content and
the ambient cabin noise, without disturbing other passengers.
Several awards in 2024 attested to the quality of this system:
◼a Good Design Award(1), in the Transport category;
◼a RedCabin Trinity Award(2) in the Passenger Experience
Concept category.
1.2.4
Competitive position
Safran covers international high-technology markets in aerospace
and defense.
In all these fields, Safran faces competition on both global and
niche markets.
The Group operates in the strictest observance of all applicable
rules on competitive business practice in all of its host
countries, complying with all specific measures applicable to
each market (see section 4.3.2.3).
To mobilize sufficient resources and share the industrial risks
involved in innovative and costly programs, participants in the
aviation industry can form partnerships capable of developing
new technologies, products and services. Accordingly, it is not
unusual to find otherwise rival companies joining forces on
certain aviation programs.
(1)
The Chicago Athenaeum (United States) Good Design Award is given in recognition of the year's most innovative industrial design products.
(2)
RedCabin (Germany) is a company that organizes trade shows bringing together leading companies from the world of transport (aviation, automotive and rail).

## Page 56

PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Investments
1.3
INVESTMENTS
1.3.1
Research & Development
Safran is a high-technology group that offers high-value-added
products and services. Technology and reliability requirements
are high, consistent with the highly critical nature of the
applications concerned. To meet these requirements, Safran
harnesses highly specialized advanced expertise in many fields:
mechanics, metals, composite materials, fuel and propulsion
systems, aerodynamics, combustion, thermodynamics, electrics,
electronics, sensors, signal processing, digital technologies,
modeling and simulation.
Research and technology (R&T) comprises all studies, research
and technological demonstrations needed to develop expertise
for a given product while optimizing its risks, costs and timeframe.
Further
downstream,
research
and
development
(R&D)
corresponds to product design, prototype manufacture,
development tests and certification tests showing that the product
meets customer specifications and applicable regulations.
The Innovation Department spans R&T and R&D to nurture the
emergence of new developments using innovative concepts
and  help Group companies identify, produce and validate
proof‑of‑concept
demonstrators,
thereby
shortening
development lead times to meet time-to-market targets.
Around 16% of Group employees are involved in R&D activities.
Safran’s
R&T
and
innovation
projects
are
guided
by
forward‑looking considerations, and the substantial budgets
they draw are in line with the Group’s current or targeted
positions in its markets (see section 1.3.1.2).
Under Safran’s R&T policy, each company in the Group
determines its own research programs for its products and
services and concentrates on its own objectives. At the same
time, the Group is constantly working to develop technology
synergies between its various businesses.
R&T operations are guided by roadmaps charting the strategic
challenges faced by Group companies (see section 1.3.1.1):
competitive positioning sought at different timeframes, along
with the corresponding technological demonstrations and
fulfillment levers in terms of external partnerships and internal
synergies. The roadmaps are analyzed on an annual basis by
Group experts, who issue recommendations accordingly.
Safran Tech is one of Safran SA’s business segments and the
Group’s R&T center. It is based in the Saclay high-tech cluster,
France’s largest science and technology campus, near Paris.
Counting around 570 employees, Safran Tech is split into four
units (energy & propulsion, materials & processes, electrical &
electronic systems, and digital sciences & technologies).
Safran  Tech also runs platforms specially equipped for
developing next-generation materials and processes: Safran
Composites,
Safran
Ceramics,
Safran
Advanced
Turbine
Airfoils and Safran Additive Manufacturing Campus.
Information on technical and scientific partnerships, innovations,
intellectual property and Safran Corporate Ventures can be
found in section 5.1.4.4 - Innovation, support to research and
scientific partnerships.
1.3.1.1
Major technological focuses
Aircraft engine technologies and new propulsion
configurations
Safran is an industry leader – due to its position in many aircraft-
system segments, including energy systems – and demonstrated
its commitment by dedicating around 88% of its self-funded
R&T budget in 2024 to reducing the environmental impact of its
products.
Efforts
are
focused
primarily
on
propulsion
architectures, as is currently the case for the RISE™ program(1)
as part of CFM International (see section 5.1.2.1), electrification,
lightweight equipment design and enabling the increasing use of
sustainable aviation fuels.
During the year, Safran made significant progress in the area of
decarbonization, notably with Open Fan engine wind-tunnel
testing as part of the RISE program and the inauguration in
Belgium of the BeCOVER test rig, the only one of its kind in
Europe, for full-scale testing of RISE compressors.
Safran’s roadmap specifies contribution to a technological
breakthrough in the form of an aircraft that consumes 30%
less  fuel compared to today’s most efficient engines.
Complemented by the widespread use of sustainable aviation
fuels, this strategy aims to contribute to achieving net-zero
emissions by 2050 (see section 5.1.2.1).
Electric technologies and new power
configurations
The increased use of electrical energy for aircraft systems and
actuators. The movement, which started with the A380 and
Boeing 787 programs, will be a defining characteristic of the next
generation of short- and medium-haul aircraft. The ultimate aim
is overall aircraft energy optimization, covering energy production
and energy consumption, for both propulsive and non-propulsive
functions. The breadth of its aircraft engine and equipment
expertise allows Safran to explore a huge spectrum of solutions
for making tomorrow’s aircraft more competitive, in terms of
performance, functionality and cost of ownership (see section 5.1.2.1).
(1)
RISE – Revolutionary Innovation for Sustainable Engines.

## Page 57

1
PRESENTATION OF THE GROUP, REVIEW OF OPERATIONS IN 2024 AND OUTLOOK FOR 2025
Investments
Materials and processes
The need to lighten planes, helicopters and their equipment has led
to increased use of composite materials, high-performance
metal alloys and additive manufacturing. Safran engines and
equipment (whether nacelles, landing gear or brakes) are
characterized by heavy mechanical loads and a more difficult
environment than for other aircraft sections such as airframes.
Safran develops its composite materials solutions through the
resources and expertise in organic matrix composites fielded at
the Safran Composites Center (part of Safran Tech). Safran
Ceramics, the Group’s center of competence for these
technologies, provides core expertise in thermostructural
composites for aircraft engines, a major technological challenge
addressed by substantial research efforts. This research, along
with work on new metal alloys, and on high-performance
coatings compliant with European REACh(1) regulations, is
coordinated by the Materials and Processes Department.
Regarding metallic materials adapted to very high running
temperatures, the platform for developing new monocrystalline
casting techniques with engine applications opened in 2019.
Safran gives priority importance to stepping up materials
research using data analysis and digital physics. The application
of statistical learning in mechanics opens up new possibilities in
the understanding of mechanisms and for ultra-fast simulations
of the properties of materials usage. With this in mind, Safran
founded the BIGMECA chair in 2019 with the Mines ParisTech
engineering school, which uses academic research for industrial
purposes. In 2021, Safran also signed a framework partnership
agreement on research and training with the École Centrale de
Lyon. This new agreement aims in particular to organize research
in the fields of aeroacoustics, aerodynamics, structural dynamics
and tribology.
R&T work on materials and processes takes into account
Safran’s technology roadmap dedicated to product recyclability
(see section 5.1.2.3).
Additive manufacturing
Additive manufacturing provides an opportunity to improve
costs, cycles and performance for numerous engine and aircraft
equipment components, by reducing the number of parts and
components and introducing new methods of optimizing
design. Certification has already been obtained for parts
including fuel injector nozzles and combustor swirlers for
helicopter engines produced via laser melting, whereby an
assembly of 15 components can be replaced by a single part.
Safran has decided to centralize all its metallic additive
manufacturing capabilities at a new Safran Additive Manufacturing
Campus. All the Group’s operations in research, industrialization
and production of additive manufacturing components are
housed at the new site in Le Haillan, near Bordeaux (France),
where more than a hundred engineers, PhD students,
technicians and other employees work to ensure optimally
efficient rollout across all relevant products.
Digital technologies and digital transformation
Industry 4.0
Product design, production, maintenance and service tools and
resources benefit from Safran’s investments in digital solutions
such as augmented reality, robotics, imaging, artificial intelligence
and data use. These new applications significantly improve
operational performance in terms of the cycle, cost and quality
of high-tech products developed, manufactured and rolled out
by the Group.
Automatic imaging solutions (based on machine learning)
developed by Safran Tech for non-destructive testing of
complex parts are already used in the Group’s plants.
Data processing
Innovation efforts are called for to address the growing role
played by services in the Group’s operations. Techniques used
to diagnose and forecast the condition of aircraft and helicopter
equipment and systems bring value for Safran product users, as
regards both operational considerations (optimization of
maintenance) and fleet management support (evaluation of
residual value). To address this need, Safran is developing its
Monitoring Services system for managing fleet equipment
operating data.
Big data extraction techniques offer promising development
opportunities. Here, the Safran Analytics teams at the Safran
Tech site, with the support and product expertise of every
Group company, will be stepping up deployment of more agile
services, better oriented to value creation for aircraft operators.
Safran Analytics has designed and rolled out its own big data
platforms to facilitate the Group-wide implementation of data
analytics solutions. One of the first services offered in these
analytics environments enabled Group companies to access
aircraft trajectory data, to improve their understanding of how
Safran products are used by customers, and thereby help them
to reduce operating costs, fuel consumption and carbon
emissions.
To boost competitive performance, Safran pursues artificial
intelligence (AI) work with ambitious plans on:
◼digital product inspection, where AI is applied to image
processing for defect recognition; and
◼generative AI, which enhances the Group's knowledge and
documentation bases, and boosts productivity.
Electronics, digital platforms and critical software
Given that onboard electronics technologies for harsh
environments are a central feature of many Group products,
Safran Electronics & Defense runs projects on controller
architectures featuring more efficient processors, and on
component packaging capable of withstanding the higher
temperature environments of future aircraft systems (see
section 1.2.2.5 – Electronics & Defense).
(1)
Registration, Evaluation and Authorisation of Chemicals.
